Title: Helios Flexi Cap Fund ups stake in HDFC Bank; exits IOCL, HPCL in March update- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The fund did not reduce positions in any stock during the month, apart from the two exits.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Samir Arora-led Helios Flexi Cap Fund increased exposure to banks, power and autos in March 2026 while exiting PSU oil companies including IOCL and HPCL, according to the fund’s latest monthly portfolio disclosure. The fund houses' flagship fund saw it’s top holdings remain concentrated in large-cap names. HDFC Bank (5.72% of AUM), Reliance Industries (5.24%) and ICICI Bank (4.25%) are the top three positions. They were followed by Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone (4.25%), State Bank of India (3.95%) and Eternal (4.10%). As of March 31, the fund house as an AUM of around Rs 5,746 crore. The fund house has continued to deploy funds and has a cash holding of around 1.42 percent. During the month of March, the fund house completely exited its position in oil marketing companies - Indian Oil Corporation Limited and Hindustan Petroleum Corporation . OMC’s have been under pressure over the last few months amidst the ongoing West Asia crisis. Brokerages expect OMCs to see sequential weaker performance, impacted by a sharp decline in gross marketing margins, forex losses etc. During the month, IOC fell nearly 24 percent while HPCL fell nearly 26 percent. During the month, the fund house made fresh positions in 4 stocks. Amidst the ongoing West Asia crisis, defence stocks have been gaining traction. In March 2026, the fund initiated a fresh position in Solar Industries, picking up 50,244 lakh shares, around 1.06 percent of the AUM. In March 2026, the stock has fallen around 13.8 percent. Most brokerages are optimistic on the stock. In its latest report, analysts at Elara Capital suggest that the company is well positioned to capture on high entry -barrier segments , such as propellants, warheads and rocket integration , ammunition, military drones & unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV ), counter -drone systems (CDS) , and anti -tank guided missiles (ATGM ). They expect a revenue CAGR of 25% and an earnings CAGR of 28% during FY25 -28E. During the month, the fund house added 208,120 lakh shares of BSE which accounts for around 0.97 percent of the AUM. The stock has gained around 1.5 percent over the last month and around 20 percent YTD. The fund also added minor positions in transportation infrastructure player GMR Airports via 4,696,000 lakh shares, accounting for around 0.69 percent of the AUM. The stock has gained around 4.5 percent over the last 6 months and fallen around 11 percent YTD. Pune-based control-intensive components maker Sedemac Mechatronics listed on March 11 with Helios MF as one of the 23 anchor investors. The stock opened at a 13 percent premium on NSE. Since listing, the stock has gained around 13.6 percent. During the month, the fund made significant additions to existing holdings in the financial space, led by HDFC Bank , where holdings rose by 12.77 lakh shares month-on-month (up 39.8%), taking its weight to around 5.72% of AUM. The stock has fallen around 16 percent over the last month amidst controversy surrounding mis-selling of high-risk Additional Tier-1 (AT1) bonds linked to Credit Suisse to NRI clients and subsequent sacking of top executives and the resignation of Chairman Atanu Chakraborty. In banks, the fund house also added 13.87 lakh shares of State Bank of India. Among cyclicals, exposure to NTPC was increased by 9.38 lakh shares. The fund also added 6.73 lakh shares of Tata Motors . Other additions included Bharti Airtel, Reliance Industries and Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone. In consumption, the fund added 12.69 lakh shares of Eternal . In conversation with CNBC TV18 on April 8, Arora noted that overall, across funds, they remain bullish. “Even large players are struggling to make money, so the pressure is reducing. At current valuations, IPOs cannot happen at the valuations they expected earlier, so they will be less aggressive. You can already see pressure easing,” he said. The fund did not reduce positions in any stock during the month, apart from the two exits. The fund house, as of March 31, 2026 has around 64 stocks, up from 62 in February 2026. In terms of sectoral allocation, banks lead at 15.6%, followed by finance (8.7%) and capital markets (8.6%). Other notable exposures include retailing (6.8%), petroleum products (5.2%), transport (4.8%), and automobiles (4.6%), with additional diversification across construction (3.7%), power (3.5%), healthcare services (3.4%), and telecom services (3.2%). Title: The Ceasefire Mirage: Price knows what headlines don’t, Bhav Bhagwan Chhe- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Markets do not reward those who chase stories. They reward those who follow data backed signals.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: A ceasefire comforts headlines, but markets are rarely fooled by optics. The pause between Iran and the United States is not the end of the conflict; it is merely an intermission in a far more complex geopolitical equation. One lesson has held true across cycles: wars may end politically, but uncertainty lingers economically and markets price that reality far more efficiently than narratives. To understand why this ceasefire lacks durability, revisit the objectives of the key stakeholders. The United States aimed to curb Iran’s nuclear ambitions and reassert dominance. Iran sought autonomy over its oil and nuclear strategy while strengthening regional leverage. Israel’s objective was clear: neutralise Iran as a strategic threat. The outcome? None of these goals has been fully achieved. What we are left with is a “non-resolution equilibrium”, a pause in conflict, not a resolution of tensions. This is why the probability of escalation remains high. Ceasefires in this region have historically been tactical, not structural. Add multiple stakeholders across the Middle East, and stability becomes inherently fragile, and all of that is not in isolation. The Russia-Ukraine war continues to keep energy markets tight. At the same time, cracks are emerging within NATO, as rising political rhetoric questions alliances. Even long-standing geopolitical frameworks are being reassessed. Europe itself is not unified. Spain’s stance on Israel and broader European discomfort add another layer of friction. More importantly, Israel appears uneasy with the ceasefire terms, having had limited involvement in negotiations. A stakeholder not aligned with an agreement is often the one that disrupts it. This is no longer a single conflict. It is a cluster of interconnected tensions the Middle East instability, Eastern European conflict, and Western alliance fragmentation all feeding into each other. So, how does one assess whether this ceasefire evolves into lasting peace? Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, war-risk insurance premiums, and most importantly, crude oil prices. Oil was near $60 pre-war and now it remains significantly elevated. Markets are clearly signalling that risk has not disappeared; it has merely been repriced. And the most critical reality remains unchanged: Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz. You can change the narrative, but not the physics of supply. Oil will continue to carry a geopolitical premium. For retail investors, the biggest risk today is confusion, not volatility. Constant news and opinions can lead to poor decisions. Instead of trying to predict events, it's essential for them to build a framework that filters out noise and focuses on signals, as markets move in response to price, liquidity, and risk perception. So instead of asking, “ What will happen next? ” Ask, “ What is the market already telling me? ” The Three Signals That will Matter the most: Crude, Yields, Gold Oil reflects geopolitical stress in real time. If crude remains elevated or rises further, it signals persistent uncertainty, higher inflation, and pressure on equities. Rising oil = market stress | Falling oil = relief Higher yields indicate tighter financial conditions. As inflation expectations rise, central banks lose flexibility, capital becomes expensive, and equity valuations compress. Gold signals declining confidence in the global system. A sustained rise in gold prices reflects a weakening dollar narrative and a shift toward a multipolar world. Gold rises not on strength, but on uncertainty and fear. This is not a time for aggressive bets, but for measured positioning. * Watch the alignment of crude, bond yields, and gold * Reduce risk when all three rise together Clarity will not come from the news. It will come from price confirmation. Narratives will keep changing. Ceasefires will be announced and questioned. Alliances will shift. But markets do not reward those who chase stories. They reward those who follow data backed signals. Because in a world full of noise, there is only one principle that consistently works as it is said in a Gujarati saying: Bhav Bhagwan Chhe - Price is God . Title: Tata Sons listing debate intensifies as Vijay Singh backs IPO push: Report- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The report added that Tata Sons, which is the principal shareholder of Tata Group companies, falls under RBI's upper-layer NBFC framework, which requires listing
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: After TVS Group's Venu Srinivasan, another senior trustee of Tata Trusts, Vijay Singh, has supported the listing of Tata Sons on the stock exchanges through an initial public offering (IPO), as reported by The Indian Express. The stance comes even as Tata Trusts had, a year earlier, passed a resolution to keep Tata Sons as an unlisted company, the newspaper noted. As per The Indian Express, Singh-former Defence Secretary and a board member of Tata Sons for 12 years until 2025-said the earlier consensus of keeping the company private may need a rethink given its expanding capital-intensive businesses. He was quoted saying, "The view over the last few years has been that Tata Sons should continue as it is, but its expansion and the new capital and technology intensive businesses do demand an urgent re-look." The report added that Tata Sons, which is the principal shareholder of Tata Group companies, falls under RBI's upper-layer NBFC framework, which requires listing. Singh was quoted stating, "Tata Sons, from its earliest days a 100 years ago, has been a key driver of nation building projects such as steel, locomotives, power and infrastructure. That role has now expanded into areas like aviation, defence, semiconductors, batteries and electronics which demand large capital that can be raised internally only up to a point," arguing that listing has become necessary. The newspaper highlighted that Tata Trusts Chairman Noel Tata is understood to favour retaining Tata Sons as a private entity. Former chairman Ratan Tata was also opposed to listing, the report added. However, Venu Srinivasan has earlier said, "A public listing would not only unlock value for minority shareholders but also equip Tata Sons with capital to sustain growth." Also Read: RBI to come out with new NBFC framework amid lack of clarity on Tata Sons' listing Citing Singh, The Indian Express reported that rising scale and strategic projects such as defence manufacturing require deeper capital access. He said, "If India is to produce fighter aircraft with a foreign partner, a huge investment will be required. Such projects, crucial for the country, should never be foregone for lack of funds which can only be raised from the market by a listed entity." Singh also pushed for greater transparency, telling The Indian Express, "Tata Sons' worth has quadrupled over the last decade and it certainly needs more transparency and regulatory oversight given the scale, value and employee strength of its businesses." Addressing concerns about ownership dilution, he said listing would not significantly alter control, adding, "I do not think listing will significantly affect the trusts which will retain their large shareholding, board seats etc and will not lose their promoter status." Also Read: TCS stock outlook: Brokerages bullish post Q4 results, see steady growth, AI tailwinds; check target prices On fears of potential takeover bids post-listing, Singh told the newspaper that such risks are limited due to the Trusts' dominant holding. He also suggested restructuring Tata Sons' articles to avoid conflicts, stating, "The articles will also need to specify that the main purpose of Tata Sons is to take the country forward through pathbreaking and cutting-edge ventures, for which it must be a listed entity," as reported by The Indian Express. The report further noted internal differences within the group, with a senior Tata Group director saying Tata Sons Chairman N Chandrasekaran views listing as essential but is constrained by the company's official position. The director told The Indian Express, "Tata Sons Chairman N Chandrasekaran sees it (listing of Tata Sons) as essential but can't say so because Tata Sons official position has been to remain private. I think some trustees will not oppose an RBI decision on listing and nor will Venu. I don't think Noel will be able to get a unanimous resolution against listing," according to the newspaper. Title: OPINION | China’s West Asia Strategy: The method in Beijing's silence - Moneycontrol.com
Description: As West Asia absorbs its worst energy shock in half a century, China's quiet response reveals a method for operating under pressure, one that India is now being tested on
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The Pakistan-brokered ceasefire announced in Islamabad on April 7 has bought the world two weeks. It has not resolved the war. The fighting paused hours before an American strike on Iran’s civilian infrastructure that US President Donald Trump had threatened in the language of civilisational annihilation. Iran has agreed to allow passage through the Strait of Hormuz under coordination with its armed forces. Pakistan has invited both sides to talks in Islamabad during the ceasefire. The oil market has exhaled. The conflict has not. This is not a war that will be settled decisively. It is a system that must be kept functioning despite it. Through six weeks that pushed physical crude benchmarks to record highs, with dated Brent assessed at $144.42 a barrel, reduced Hormuz traffic to a fraction of normal levels, and created what the International Energy Agency calls a shock of historic scale, one major power has said little and done much. China has been among the quietest voices in the crisis and one of the better buffered major powers in its outcome. That silence is worth reading carefully. The method inside it is legible. Calibration is the practice of adjusting multiple pressures at once without forcing a single outcome. Each step is measured and connected to the rest. It is the operating logic of actors who understand that the system around them is more valuable than any single result within it. Small, open, trade-dependent jurisdictions learn it early. Singapore has practised a version of it for decades. China has scaled that logic to continental proportions. At the United Nations, China and Russia vetoed a resolution on shipping security in the Strait of Hormuz that they judged would narrow Tehran’s room for manoeuvre. China called for de-escalation while keeping channels open with Riyadh, Abu Dhabi and Doha. Chinese refiners remained a key outlet for Iranian crude, returning as buyers when pricing again became attractive. During the worst weeks of the closure, Iran’s Revolutionary Guard operated a toll system in the strait with fees accepted in Chinese yuan or stablecoins, according to Bloomberg and Lloyd’s List; China’s Ministry of Commerce acknowledged the reporting. This is what calibration looks like in practice. Limited moves, aligned in direction, designed to preserve room rather than force outcomes. The room being preserved is specific: continued access to Iranian crude at working prices, continued commercial standing with Gulf producers hosting American forces, continued technology and logistics links with Israel, and continued freedom from any security commitment that would expose Chinese assets to a conflict Beijing had no intention of owning. China’s position is reinforced by its economic ties across the region. It is a leading trading partner for Gulf economies, including Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, with bilateral trade with each exceeding $100 billion in recent years. China has also maintained commercial links with Israel in technology and logistics, even as political positions diverge. Whether Beijing was also working the back channel through Islamabad or Tehran is a question no open source can settle, but the method holds either way. China has not controlled the sequence of escalation, retaliation and ceasefire that has unfolded in this conflict. It has managed its exposure by keeping its main channels open while avoiding commitments that would narrow its options. The ceasefire reflects this dynamic. It is not a resolution imposed by a single power. It is a pause shaped by market pressure, energy risk and diplomacy. It shows how the system holds under strain. It does not remove it. Some have described China as an unintended winner. There is some truth in that. The United States is absorbed in crisis management. China gains time. Its exposure rises with every escalation. Energy security becomes more fragile. Relations with Washington tighten. Gulf partners continue to engage China as a customer and investor but also question its role in stabilising conditions. China has not won. It has bought time, preserved room, and absorbed risk. What this episode reveals is how the system now works. Flows do not stop when politics harden. They are rerouted and supported by those who need them to continue. The constraint is whether access remains usable. For India, this is not a distant event. It is a direct test. India imports close to 90 percent of its oil. Around 40 percent moves through the Strait of Hormuz. As tensions rose, shipping insurance costs surged sharply. New Delhi moved to support insurers and keep trade functioning under higher risk. India’s conduct has been capable. The instincts are sound. The question is whether those instincts harden into a method. Calibration is not a posture adopted during a crisis. It is a capability built before the crisis arrives and sustained through the next one. It requires the discipline to hold multiple relationships without collapsing any of them, to keep financial and shipping channels usable under stress, and to refuse the choices that would narrow future options. China’s approach is undergirded by perseverance China built this reserve deliberately. The 25-year comprehensive strategic partnership with Iran, signed in 2021, established the architecture now underpinning its energy access. Parallel partnerships with Saudi Arabia and the UAE gave Beijing standing on both sides of the same rivalry. In 2023, China brokered the normalisation between Saudi Arabia and Iran, demonstrating that it could hold both relationships without collapsing either. What looks like silence today is the output of a decade of preparation. India has the standing, the scale and the relationships to build the same reserve. This crisis has shown that the instincts are already in place. The work ahead is the architecture that turns instinct into capability, and the discipline to treat a crisis response as the first draft of a method rather than its completion. The ceasefire will be renegotiated. The conflict will not end with it. The lesson is not how the pause was brokered. It is how a major power moved through the worst energy shock in half a century with its options intact and its commitments limited. It is less visible than victory, but far more durable. (Robin Hu is Emeritus Asia Chair of Milken Institute and Advisory Senior Director at Temasek.) Views are personal, and do not represent the stand of this publication. Title: Gold, Silver Rates Today (Apr. 10) - Highlights: Gold holds steady at Rs 1,52,775, silver rises marginally up; precious metals outlook
Description: Bullion traded marginally lower on early April 10 trade amid easing tensions in West Asia, while investors closely track geopolitical developments following the US and Iran's decision to agree to a two-week ceasefire.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: See the top gainers, losers, invest and get updated what's happening in the crypto market Discover the secret world of unlisted shares — analyse India's top pre-IPO companies and invest before they hit the markets Bullion traded marginally lower on early April 10 trade amid easing tensions in West Asia, while investors closely track geopolitical developments following the US and Iran's decision to agree to a two-week ceasefire. IBJA pegs gold at Rs 1,49,937, silver at Rs 2,36,158 The Indian Bullion Jewellers Association pegged the standard price of 10 grams of gold at Rs 1,49,937, down 0.78 percent from the previous session. Silver declined 3.23 percent to Rs 2,36,158 per kilogram in its 18:30 rate session. Meanwhile, the MCX futures gold traded 0.43 percent down to Rs 1,52,775 per 10 gm, and silver declined 0.16 percent to Rs 2,43,376 per 1 kg (19:00 IST). Gold hold steady after brief dip; silver crosses Rs 2.41 lakh The domestic futures gold price on MCX fell to an intraday low around Rs 1,51,746 per 10 grams, but quickly gained some of those losses to trade at Rs 1,52,654 per 10 gm of 24-karat purity from its previous close, though trading in red with a 0.51 percent decline. Silver climbed briefly to around Rs 2,43,440 per kilogram, but trimmed those gains to trade at Rs 2,41,533 per kg, representing a 0.92 percent decline (17:12 IST). President Trump expressed optimism over a potential deal to end the six-week conflict, but also threatened Iran over proposed fees for passage through the Strait of Hormuz, a move that reintroduced a risk premium into energy and bullion markets. Separately, Israeli strikes in Lebanon have further destabilised the fragile ceasefire brokered earlier this week, keeping geopolitical uncertainty elevated and safe-haven demand structurally supported. Gold steady above Rs 1.52 lakh, silver surge past Rs 2.41 lakh The domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.65 percent lower to Rs 1,52,444 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 0.95 percent lower at Rs 2,41,448 per kilogram (16:08 IST). Analysts noted that central bank accumulation continues to provide a strong floor for gold demand. Poland's central bank purchased 20 tonnes in February, maintaining its strategic target of 700 tonnes in total reserves. China added approximately 5 tonnes in March, its largest single monthly purchase in over a year, capitalising on a price dip. This sustained institutional buying signals that global reserve managers view current levels as strategically attractive, reinforcing gold's medium-term bullish outlook regardless of short-term geopolitical noise. The domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.82 percent lower at Rs 1,52,181 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 1.4 percent lower at Rs 2,40,352 per kilogram (15:20 IST). The Augmont report noted that bullion prices have recovered firmly, supported by cautious diplomatic optimism on the Iran conflict and sustained central bank buying. However, the upside remains capped by fragile ceasefire conditions. A breakdown in negotiations could pull gold back to the mid-$4,000s swiftly, while a credible peace deal would clear the path toward $5000 — making the current range a pivotal decision zone for markets. Gold 0.74% down to Rs 1,52,300, silver declines 1.04% The Augmont report (Apr. 10) estimates that if gold sustains above Rs 1,53,000, the next upside resistance would be Rs 1,59,000. Similarly, if silver moves above Rs 2,44,000, the next resistance would be Rs 2,55,000 and Rs 2,65,000 on continued strength. Currently, the domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.74 percent lower at Rs 1,52,300 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 1.04 percent lower at Rs 2,41,2228 per kilogram (13:27 IST). Gold trades 0.51% lower to Rs 1,52,647 lakh, silver edges 0.63% down The domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.51 percent lower at Rs 1,52,647 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 0.63 percent lower at Rs 2,42,262 per kilogram (11:42 IST). A softer US dollar and rising expectations of monetary easing amid recession concerns support flows into safe-haven assets. Analysts say that the market remains highly sensitive to geopolitical developments, with uncertainty around the durability of the ceasefire acting as a key overhang. Gold trades 0.48% lower at Rs 1,52,693 lakh, silver edges 0.47% down The domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.48 percent lower at Rs 1,52,693 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 0.47 percent lower at Rs 2,42,613 per kilogram (10:32 IST). A softer US dollar and rising expectations of monetary easing amid recession concerns support flows into safe-haven assets. Gold trades 0.05% lower at Rs 1,52,671 lakh, silver edges 0.56% down The domestic futures gold price on MCX traded 0.05 percent lower at Rs 1,52,671 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 0.56 percent lower at Rs 2,42,392 per kilogram (9:40 IST) on the second day of the ceasefire between the US and Iran. According to analysts, Iran accepting tolls in Bitcoin, stablecoins, or Chinese yuan to let shipments pass through the Strait of Hormuz indicates a workaround for an alternate payment system, which could benefit nations globally, looking for instant settlements on record. Gold opens MCX 0.05% lower at Rs 1,52,663 lakh, silver edges 1.51% down The domestic futures gold price on MCX opened the Friday session 0.05 percent lower at Rs 1,52,663 per 10 grams of 24-karat purity from its previous close, and silver edged 1.51 percent lower at Rs 2,42,525 per kilogram amid the second day of the ceasefire between the US and Iran. However, the current ceasefire is only a short-term truce, with claims from both sides that it has already been violated, amid confusion over whether Lebanon is included in the ceasefire agreement. It is very fragile, and markets could question its durability, suggesting gold could remain supported by cautious investors. Title: Chartist Talk: Crude oil’s next leg of upside hinges on a break above $120–123 a barrel, says Ashish Kyal- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Ashish Kyal believes the low of 22,180 is going to be crucial for Nifty 50 because, if one looks at the weekly timeframe chart, this is the same level that was seen during May 2024. A very important base formation also happened in this zone in March 2025.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: "The market is entering into crucial week ahead. Break below 22,180 on Nifty 50 can intensify selling pressure but any breach of 22,942 will indicate short term low in place for upside pullbacks," said Ashish Kyal, the Founder and CEO of Waves Strategy Advisors in an interview to Moneycontrol. According to him, for crude oil prices to start next leg on upside, it needs to see the prices crossing above $120-123 a barrel hurdle zone. Below $97 levels, crude can move against the majority’s logical understanding which is on downside due to demand disruptions that many are ignoring, he said. Are the charts signalling that the Nifty 50 is likely to break 22,000 and even the April low of 21,700 next week? Nifty is moving in the form of gaps. Prices are moving sharply, on average, by around 500 points. However, in the prior trading session, after a big gap-down opening, Nifty made a low around 22,180 and managed to reverse on the upside, filling the gap. We believe the low of 22,180 is going to be crucial because, if we look at the weekly timeframe chart, this is the same level that was seen during May 2024. A very important base formation also happened in this zone in March 2025. We are currently observing some supporting activity around this level. However, we are yet to see strong positive weekly momentum. This will only come on a break above 22,942 levels, which is the weekly high of the prior week. So, in the short term, volatility can remain high, but there is a likelihood that the 22,180 to 22,000 zone may remain protected. A break above 22,940 will be the first sign of a reversal towards 23,450 levels. Are FII positions strengthening your bearish view? FII selling has intensified, particularly since the end of February 2026. Additionally, the increase in charges—especially the revised STT (Securities Transaction Tax) announced by the Finance Minister on Budget Day has come into effect from April 1, 2026, impacting market participation and overall liquidity. Despite this selloff, DIIs have played a supportive role, with their buying activity picking up significantly, especially during the month of March 2026. We are entering into crucial week for the markets. Break below 22,180 can intensify selling pressure but any breach of 22,942 will indicate short term low in place for upside pullbacks. Do you see a possibility of the Bank Nifty breaking the 200-week EMA (48,850) as well as the March 2025 lows next week? Bank Nifty is currently protecting its crucial 200-week EMA, while banking stocks have remained under pressure. The index has already corrected over 18 percent and is now entering an oversold zone, with the weekly RSI slipping to 29 levels which we have not seen since April 2020. Despite the weakness, the 200-week EMA remains a strong long-term support which is near 48,850. Given the extended downside short term pullback is possible as long as 49,950 remains intact. Break above 52,000 will be a sign of positive reversal for retracement to 53,600 levels. What are the possible scenarios that could change your view from bearish to bullish? A potential reversal in both Nifty and Bank Nifty can be identified using a simple price action rule on weekly time frame. We need to see a weekly close above prior weekly high with low above prior low. Since 8 weeks Nifty has not shown close above prior weekly high. So by end of the week if prices close above 22,942 we will get first positive indication. Are the charts indicating that oil prices may see a new leg of an upmove soon? Brent crude on March 9 touched the high of $119.50 a barrel level. This is near to the highs seen during Russia-Ukraine war in 2022. Crude since then has still not broken this high despite of all the disruption seen because of war. So for Crude to start next leg on upside we need to see it cross above $120 – 123 hurdle zone. Below $97 levels, crude can move against the majority’s logical understanding which is on downside due to demand disruptions that many are ignoring. Which two stocks are on your radar for next week? Adani Power has confirmed a breakout from a rounding bottom pattern, supported by a notable surge in volumes indicating strong buying interest. The stock also recently rebounded from its upward-sloping trendline, reinforcing the bullish setup. Any dip towards the neckline around Rs 158 level can be seen as a buying opportunity, with potential upside targets placed in the Rs 168–174 range with important support being around Rs 150 level. Anand Rathi Wealth has continued to move up with each session passing by despite weakness in majority of indices suggesting relative outperformance. Over the past six trading sessions, it has consistently held above the previous day’s low on a closing basis, indicating sustained buying interest. Currently, the stock is approaching its prior swing high at Rs 3,241. A decisive breakout above this level is crucial to maintain bullish momentum with the possible upside targets of Rs 3,450-3,500 as long as Rs 3,070 level remains protected on the downside. Do you see the rally continuing in Bosch and Page Industries next week? Bosch has reversed sharply from its support zone of Rs 28,646 which is a 200-week EMA support. The stock has not only recovered its prior two weeks’ losses but has also gained over 12 percent from the support levels which is a positive sign. The trend is likely to continue with some minor dips along the way with targets of Rs 33,500 followed by Rs 34,600 levels. The view is valid as long as Rs 30,400 remains protected on the downside. Page Industries has formed a rounding bottom pattern at the lows signaling a potential trend change. The stock is also showing a formation of higher highs and higher lows with Bollinger Bands expanding which is sign of increase in volatility. For now, a break above Rs 34,070 can extend this rise further which can push prices higher towards Rs 36,300-36,900 levels with Rs 32,400 as an important support. Title: CLSA turns constructive on Indian equities after 18 months; shifts to alpha strategy- Moneycontrol.com
Description: CLSA exited ITC in favour of Varun Beverages and replaced Bajaj Auto with Mahindra and Mahindra. It prefers Vedanta over UltraTech Cement in a post-war scenario and sees Larsen and Toubro as a recovery play, replacing defensive exposure to NTPC.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Brokerage CLSA has turned constructive on Indian equities after nearly 18 months of a bearish stance, saying the risk-reward profile has now balanced as markets appear to have moved past the peak impact of the Iran conflict. The brokerage said sentiment had reached extreme bearishness, while valuations on both absolute and relative basis were trading below 10-year averages, indicating a more favourable entry point for investors. CLSA added that its portfolio outperformed the Nifty by 6 percentage points in the March quarter, but it has now moved away from a beta-controlled strategy to a more traditional alpha-seeking approach. As part of portfolio changes, CLSA exited ITC in favour of Varun Beverages and replaced Bajaj Auto with Mahindra and Mahindra. It prefers Vedanta over UltraTech Cement in a post-war scenario and sees Larsen and Toubro as a recovery play, replacing defensive exposure to NTPC. The brokerage also sees better risk-reward in Bajaj Finance compared to IndusInd Bank. CLSA has raised its overweight position on financials and cut IT to underweight, swapping Tech Mahindra for HDFC Bank. It continues to hold ICICI Bank, State Bank of India, ONGC, Tata Motors, Infosys, Avenue Supermarts, Eternal and Godrej Properties in its portfolio. On the macro front, CLSA expects crude oil prices to remain elevated above pre-war levels for the next few months, likely settling in the range of $80 to $90 per barrel, driven by inventory restocking, gradual resumption of production and normalisation of shipping. As a result, inflation risks may remain slightly above pre-war levels, keeping Indian bond yields elevated. However, the brokerage does not expect any rate hikes from the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), while noting that the government may have limited fiscal room. Globally, CLSA expects a return to pre-war trends, with a weaker dollar supporting flows into emerging markets and select commodities. This could improve capital flows into India and ease pressure on the rupee. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: US-Iran ceasefire to cool jet fuel prices, bring relief to airlines- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Jet fuel prices have surged since the onset of the Iran war, tracking a spike in global crude and refining margins
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The two-week US and Iran ceasefire announced on April 8 morning is expected to cool soaring jet fuel prices, offering much-needed relief to aviation companies grappling with a surge in costs and were forced to introduce fuel surcharges to cushion the impact. Aviation turbine fuel (ATF) prices have risen sharply since the onset of the war, triggering a spike in price of crude following supply disruptions. On April 1, the price surged to a record high of over Rs 2.07 lakh per kilolitre, with base prices increasing by nearly 25 percent. Fuel account for 30–50 percent of airlines’ operating expenses in India. The government stepped in to shield domestic carriers, capping the increase to about 8.5 percent, or roughly Rs 1.05 lakh a kilolitre. However, international and charter operators bore the brunt of the surge, with prices soaring 115 percent to over Rs 2.07 lakh per kilolitre. The situation will likely ease. Benchmark Brent crude oil prices slumped more than 15 percent after the ceasefire announcement was trading around $95 a barrel, the lowest in recent weeks. The truce, which US President Donald Trump said was conditional to safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery for global energy shipments, has raised hopes of improved oil flows. Industry executives said the cooling crude could gradually translate into lower ATF rates, though the pass-through may not be immediate due to supply-chain disruptions and elevated refining margins. Within days of the war, refining margins surged nearly tenfold, even as crude rose more moderately. The combined effect of higher crude and a spike in Platts premiums pushed ATF prices up by nearly 160 percent in under a week to about $225 a barrel, according to industry officials. Airlines responded with calibrated fare hikes, though executives maintain these measures offered only limited relief. Full-service carrier Air India on March 10 introduced a flat Rs 399 fuel surcharge on domestic routes, along with $20–$30 on Southeast Asia and Africa sectors and $25–$50 on long-haul routes to Europe, North America and Australia. From April 8, the airline revised its domestic surcharge to a distance-based structure, ranging from Rs 299 for flights up to 500 km to Rs 899 for sectors beyond 2,000 km. On international routes, however, surcharges have seen a sharp escalation, rising 40–65 percent on key US and Europe sectors. Low-cost carrier IndiGo also implemented a revised fuel surcharge structure effective April 2, allowing it to charge between Rs 275 and Rs 1,200 on domestic routes and Rs 900 to Rs 10,000 on international sectors. Despite these increases, airlines acknowledge that fare hikes have barely offset the surge in fuel costs. “The fuel surcharge is a very small component in the overall cost structure. On domestic routes, it covers only about 17 percent of the additional cost. The remaining 83 percent is being absorbed,” an Air India official said, cautioning that a full pass-through would render air travel prohibitively expensive. Brokerage estimates reinforce this view. Analysts at Jefferies said IndiGo’s surcharge could lift yields by just Rs 0.30–Rs 0.35 per seat against an annual base of about Rs 5.1. Meanwhile, Citigroup estimates a relatively higher—but still constrained—yield impact of 8–10 percent. While the ceasefire has triggered optimism across the aviation sector, executives remain cautious. Any sustained cooling in ATF prices will depend on the durability of the truce and the normalization of oil supply chains. For now, the ceasefire offers a breather—but not a complete reset—for an industry still navigating one of its sharpest fuel cost shocks in recent years. Title: US-Iran ceasefire brings breathing room for electronics makers amid supply chain fears- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Niche inputs such as helium and petrochemical derivatives—both critical for semiconductor and electronics manufacturing—remain exposed to supply shocks linked to geopolitical tensions.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The ceasefire between the United States and Iran is expected to ease immediate risks to global supply chains, bringing relief to India’s electronics manufacturers who had been bracing for disruptions triggered by the West Asia conflict. Industry players said they are continuing to closely monitor commodity prices and potential supply shocks, even as their current margins remain largely protected through long-term contracts and pass-through clauses. “We are monitoring the situation since it’s a two-week pause in hostilities between the US and Iran, but it will be a positive for the sector if a long-term solution is reached,” a top executive with a leading electronics maker told Moneycontrol. Analysts said the announcement of a two-week pause in hostilities has calmed nerves, while also highlighting lingering uncertainty for the semiconductor ecosystem, particularly OSAT facilities and the broader electronics value chain. “This has calmed down a lot of nerves and created uncertainty across sectors, including electronics. The window will also give space to a diplomatic solution. The short term impact is positive, especially easing fears on the fuel prices. This will help to stabilize prices, but markets are likely to operate cautiously till a permanent solution emerges,” Tarun Pathak told Moneycontrol. Niche inputs such as helium and petrochemical derivatives—both critical for semiconductor and electronics manufacturing—remain exposed to supply shocks linked to geopolitical tensions. While the ceasefire reduces the immediate risk of disruption, industry participants expect some lag before supply chains fully stabilise. For India’s electronics sector, the conflict phase had triggered volatility in crude oil prices and disrupted key shipping routes, raising input and logistics costs for manufacturers dependent on imported components. Moneycontrol reported on March 18 that due to the US–Israel–Iran conflict disrupting West Asia, Apple made changes to its global supply chain, rerouting India-made iPhone shipments away from the Middle East—particularly Dubai in the United Arab Emirates—and redirecting them to alternative hubs, marking a sharp shift from one of its most heavily used transit corridors. The Middle East, particularly hubs like Dubai and Qatar, plays a central role in global smartphone logistics, enabling rapid redistribution to Europe, Africa and the US. Disruptions have forced rerouting via longer corridors across Central Asia, East Asia and Africa, increasing flight times, fuel consumption and operating costs. Moneycontrol reported on March 26 that India’s semiconductor and printed circuit board (PCB) industry is staring at a critical supply risk after Iran’s March 19 strike on Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG hub disrupted a key global source of helium—an essential, non-substitutable input for chip fabrication. The shock threatens to ripple through manufacturing lines just as India accelerates its ambitions of becoming a global electronics hub, exposing a key vulnerability in its supply chain. Industry executives had said that many fabs, which run 24 hours a day, cut down operations to 16 hours depending on gas availability, directly impacting manufacturing output. While this has not triggered an outright chip shortage like during the Covid-19 pandemic, it has significantly extended lead times. They had also warned of an impact on India’s PCB manufacturing ecosystem, which is still in the early stages of capacity build-out, due to the hostilities. “If disruptions continue over an extended period beyond this pause of two weeks, we could see pricing pressures and some tightening of supply. Over time, that could translate into chip shortages, which would then impact electronics production more broadly," another senior industry executive said. Brokerage firm Kotak Institutional Equities, in a March 6 note, flagged mobile phones among the top five commodities exposed to disruptions from the West Asia conflict, warning that prolonged tensions could significantly dent export value to the region in the next fiscal year. The ceasefire may also bring some relief on the demand side. Cooling fuel prices can ease inflationary pressures, potentially supporting discretionary spending on electronics such as smartphones, televisions and appliances, analysts said. Title: Scratch cards to state power: Lottery baron Santiago Martin's controversial rise; wife Leema Rose among TN's richest candidates- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Martin's wife, Leema Rose Martin, is among the richest candidates in Tamil Nadu, with declared assets of around Rs 1,041 crore.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: An Indian businessman who built a vast lottery empire from humble beginnings has come under renewed national scrutiny after being identified as the single largest donor under India’s now-scrapped electoral bond scheme. Santiago Martin’s company, Future Gaming and Hotel Services Pvt. Ltd, purchased electoral bonds worth Rs 13.68 billion between April 2019 and January 2024. The disclosures came after the Supreme Court struck down the scheme and ordered publication of donor identities. The electoral bond system, introduced to enable anonymous political donations, was widely criticised for reducing transparency in political funding. While the contributions were legal, the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), which introduced the scheme, emerged as the biggest beneficiary overall, receiving nearly half of all bonds issued between 2018 and 2024. Of the bonds purchased by Martin’s company, more than Rs 5 billion reportedly went to the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK), which governs Tamil Nadu, where Martin built his business. The remaining recipients have not yet been fully identified. Martin’s origins can be traced to the Andaman Islands, where he was born in 1961. As a teenager, he worked as a daily-wage labourer in present-day Myanmar before returning to India in the 1980s, where he worked in a tea shop in Tamil Nadu. Observing the popularity of lottery tickets among the public, especially low-income groups, he launched his first lottery outlet in Coimbatore. Within a few years, he expanded rapidly and overtook competitors to become Tamil Nadu’s leading lottery distributor. His “two-digit” lottery system, involving scratch cards matched against televised draws, became widely popular and later expanded into Karnataka, Kerala, northern India and even Bhutan. In a 2001 interview with rediff.com , Martin had said his company sold around 12 million tickets daily. “I succeeded because I grasped the psychology of the buyer and the tricks of the trade,” he said. Martin’s rise has been accompanied by long-standing controversy. His associates have repeatedly denied allegations of lottery fraud, including claims of rigged draws and irregular prize claims. In 2003, Tamil Nadu banned lotteries under then Chief Minister J. Jayalalithaa, citing reports of widespread financial ruin among participants. The move dealt a major blow to his business. In 2011, during Jayalalithaa’s tenure, Martin was arrested in a land-grabbing case and spent several months in jail before being released on bail. In 2023, India’s financial crimes agency seized assets and bank deposits worth Rs 4.5 billion following searches linked to alleged lottery fraud in Sikkim. His appeal against the seizure was later dismissed, although he has not been convicted in any of the cases. His company, however, maintains on its website that Future Gaming “is known for its compliance towards rules and regulations” in all jurisdictions where it operates. Martin’s business has repeatedly intersected with politics. In 2007, controversy erupted in Kerala after a communist party newspaper returned a Rs 20 million donation from him amid criticism of lottery-related social harm. At the time, Kerala’s then Chief Minister VS Achuthanandan had launched a crackdown on illegal lotteries, citing severe financial distress and reported suicides linked to losses. In Tamil Nadu, allegations have surfaced over political funding links. In 2019, DMK leader M. K. Stalin filed a defamation case after a magazine claimed he had negotiated a Rs 5 billion donation deal with Martin. Stalin denied the allegation, calling it “a figment of imagination.” In 2011, Martin also produced a Tamil-language film based on Maxim Gorky’s novel Mother, with a screenplay written by then-DMK leader and former Chief Minister M Karunanidhi. The film reportedly had a budget of around Rs 200 million but received poor reviews. The electoral bond revelations have reignited political debate in Tamil Nadu over donations to the DMK, especially given the state’s ban on lotteries. The party has denied receiving any undue favour and has not responded to recent queries. Martin’s family has also emerged as a political and financial force. His wife, Leema Rose Martin, is among the richest candidates in Tamil Nadu politics, with declared assets of around Rs 1,041 crore. Her affidavit reportedly lists total assets of Rs 1,049 crore, including movable assets of Rs 139.62 crore and immovable assets of Rs 909.94 crore. The family’s overall wealth, as declared in filings, places Martin’s net worth at approximately Rs 4,149 crore, with movable assets of Rs 3,262 crore and immovable assets of Rs 887.36 crore. Leema Rose has entered electoral politics and is contesting as a candidate of the AIADMK in Tamil Nadu Assembly Elections this year. Her husband’s business empire spans lottery operations, real estate, hospitality, software and media-related ventures. Despite repeated allegations and multiple investigations, Santiago Martin has not been convicted in any case. He has not publicly commented on the electoral bond disclosures. Title: Personal Loan | Latest & Breaking News on Personal Loan | Photos, Videos, Breaking Stories and Articles on Personal Loan
Description: Get all latest & breaking news on Personal Loan. Watch videos, top stories and articles on Personal Loan at moneycontrol.com.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: See the top gainers, losers, invest and get updated what's happening in the crypto market Discover the secret world of unlisted shares — analyse India's top pre-IPO companies and invest before they hit the markets A personal loan is a fixed amount borrowed from a banking or non-banking entity for just about any purpose. It can be paid back in monthly installments More You’re earning well, but the bank is looking at a very different version of your finances. Lower monthly payments feel manageable, but stretching your loan often means paying far more than you realise. A personal loan rejection can be discouraging, but it also offers an opportunity to reassess and improve your financial profile. Understanding the reasons behind the rejection, improving your credit score and waiting before reapplying can significantly boost your chances of approval. Taking a strategic approach ensures a smoother and more successful loan application the next time. Self-employed individuals can now access personal loans with proper incomes proofs. With digital platforms simplifying the process, quick and paperless loan approvals have become more accessible than ever. Working at a startup or an unlisted company doesn’t automatically prevent you from getting a personal loan. Maintaining good financial discipline can improve your chances of quick approval and better loan terms. Instant loan apps make borrowing quick and convenient but it varies across lenders. While prepaying can help, borrowers should carefully review all details before proceeding. Choosing transparent platforms and understanding the fine print is key to making smarter financial decisions. Managing both an education loan and a personal loan can be challenging, especially with higher interest costs and reduced financial flexibility. While it’s possible to take a personal loan during education loan repayment, it’s important to evaluate certain factors. It’s not just about saving interest — it’s about what you can stick to for years. Your credit utilisation ratio plays a key role in determining your personal loan eligibility, as it reflects how responsibly you manage your credit. Keeping utilisation below 30% can improve your credit score and increase your chances of securing a higher loan amount with better terms. Your credit card history can play a key role in unlocking better personal loan deals, from lower interest rates to higher loan amounts. Responsible usage helps build a strong credit profile that lenders reward. Platforms like Moneycontrol make it easy to compare instant, paperless loan offers tailored to your eligibility. Before agreeing to act as a guarantor, a retiree must know that a guarantee is not a symbolic assurance but a legally enforceable obligation that can expose the guarantor to the entire debt Personal loans are easy to access today, but they work best in certain situations rather than as a routine way to fund spending. Prepaying a loan feels financially responsible, but in some situations it barely saves money. Gold is a popular safe-haven during uncertainty and short-term cash needs, especially when emergency funds are exhausted and it serves as a hedge in your portfolio, but add risks when prices are high and volatile. You didn’t do anything “wrong”. You just don’t look comfortable to a bank. Both let you borrow quickly, but they behave very differently once interest starts ticking. They both promise easy monthly payments, but the maths under the hood is very different. It can lower your EMI on paper, but unless the timing and costs line up, refinancing often just reshuffles debt rather than reducing it. Most personal loans are not about luxury. They are about pressure, timing and the gap between what you have and what you suddenly need. A practical, honest look at what really happens when you apply and what you should check before borrowing. Compare costs, EMIs, interest rates and flexibility before choosing between premium financing firms, credit cards or personal loans to fund your insurance payment Both options have advantages. The right choice depends on your cash flow, goals and comfort with risk Reducing your principal early is the simplest way to save interest. Yet most borrowers focus only on the EMI. A few steady habits can make repayment feel manageable instead of overwhelming. It’s not impossible to borrow without salary slips, but it’s rarely simple and almost never cheap. Title: Indian stocks enter capitulation zone; is this panic setting up the next rally?- Moneycontrol.com
Description: With over 70% of stocks below their 200-day moving average, the investment firm says extreme fear may be laying the ground for a rebound.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The Indian equity market has been through a rollercoaster during the past few weeks, facing structural shocks from the trade disruptions due to the conflict in West Asia, with many stocks falling sharply. A report by Vallum Capital suggests that the recent nosedive is a sign of speedy recovery in the market. According to the report, the market has reached a point of "capitulation", where fear is overriding fundamentals, and this is often a precursor to a strong recovery. "When more than 70% of stocks fall below their 200-day moving average, it's a sign of extreme stress," said Vallum Capital. On April 8, this reading hit 71.3%, indicating that the market is in a "Capitulation Zone". Historically, this has been a rewarding entry point, with median 1-year forward returns of +17.5%, according to the investment firm. The report also highlighted the unprecedented velocity of normalisation in energy prices. Crude oil shocks usually take around 30 weeks to stabilise, but this time it took just 9 weeks. "This accelerated stabilisation is a massive signal for broader market stability," said Vallum Capital. The report also notes that India's price-to-earnings (PE) premium over Emerging Markets (EM) has undergone a significant compression, making Indian stocks relatively cheap. "The philosophy that 'price always matters' is being validated," said Vallum Capital. Vallum Capital added that structural reforms are often born in the "crucible of crisis", and the current geopolitical conflict has sparked a similar evolution in the defence sector. India has achieved record defence exports and has a huge pipeline of investments in the sector. "Markets often remain in a flat 'time-correction' zone for two years to provide a concentrated reward in the third," said Vallum Capital. With the "Max Fear" phase subsiding, the report suggests that investors should start looking at the opportunities ahead. "The question for investors is no longer about the risks of the battlefield, but whether they are positioned for the "New Innings" as the structural leaders of the next cycle emerge," Vallum Capital said. The report suggests that investors should look at sectors that have shown resilience during the turmoil, such as defence, and be prepared for a potential recovery in the coming months. Title: Buzzing stocks: Earnings, deals and corporate updates drive movement in TCS, Wipro, Ola Electric- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Several stocks remained in focus on April 10 as corporate developments, earnings updates and regulatory triggers drove market activity.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Several stocks remained in focus on April 10 as corporate developments, earnings updates and regulatory triggers drove market activity. Shares of Tata Consultancy Services fell 2 percent after the company reported its March-quarter results. The IT major reported fourth-quarter revenue of $7.6 billion, up 1.2 percent quarter-on-quarter in constant currency terms and 0.8 percent in organic constant currency terms, largely in line with estimates. However, full-year revenue declined 2.4 percent year-on-year in constant currency terms. Growth in the quarter was led by the energy, utilities and consumer businesses. Wipro gained 1.5 percent after the company said its board will consider a proposal for a share buyback along with its fourth-quarter results on April 16. IFB Industries rose 2.5 percent after the company announced key leadership changes. The board approved the appointment of Manoj Kumar Vijay as an additional non-executive independent director for two years. It also appointed Sandeep Joseph Abraham as managing director and chief executive officer for a five-year term. Shares of Ola Electric Mobility extended gains for the second straight session, rising 20 percent to Rs 36.34. The rally followed positive developments in the company’s battery technology, with its in-house developed lithium iron phosphate cell nearing readiness, pending regulatory approvals. Sun Pharmaceutical Industries declined 3 percent after ET reports that the company is proceeding with a binding offer of $12 billion to acquire Organon & Co, marking one of its largest overseas acquisition attempts. Banking stocks traded higher, with Axis Bank rising 2 percent, ICICI Bank gaining 1.6 percent and State Bank of India advancing 1.5 percent. Market participants said stock-specific news flow remains the key driver in the current environment, with investors closely tracking corporate announcements and sector-specific triggers. With the earnings season underway, company-specific developments are expected to continue driving market volatility. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: OPINION | India’s new pesticide bill must keep pace with emerging export challenges- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Indian agriculture faces supply disruptions and rising input costs. Global pesticide rules are getting stricter. This calls for faster reforms and better access to modern crop protection technologies
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Indian farmers and exporters are facing a twin challenge today. On one hand, disruptions in West Asia are affecting trade routes and agricultural supply chains. Rice shipments have slowed in recent weeks, with inventories building up at mills as transit risks increase. Input costs are also rising. Global fertiliser markets have tightened due to supply disruptions, and the domestic crop protection industry has indicated a 20–25% increase in pesticide input costs linked to higher logistics and raw material pressures. Rising Global Standards and Market Access Risks Alongside these immediate pressures, a more structural challenge is emerging. Pesticide residue standards are tightening globally, particularly in the European Union, where Indian consignments of rice, spices and other produce have faced repeated scrutiny and, in some cases, rejections at the border. Unlike short-term disruptions, these changes are regulatory in nature and are likely to shape long-term market access. This comes at a time when the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare is expected to table the draft Pesticides Management Bill 2025 in the Parliament. The Bill seeks to replace a decades-old regulatory framework and modernise how the crop protection sector is governed. The timing is significant. As recently as 7 th March 2026, the EU began applying stricter residue limits for certain neonicotinoids widely used in domestic tea cultivation. Industry bodies in Assam have indicated that this could affect exports of about 40 million kilograms, worth roughly USD 140 million. The context becomes sharper given that India has concluded a Free Trade Agreement with the EU. While the agreement eases tariffs across several agricultural products, sanitary and phytosanitary standards in the EU continue to tighten. Rejections of Indian non-basmati rice consignments in the EU have risen sharply in recent years. Increasingly, these standards are determining market access, despite easing tariffs through the deal. Recent policy changes in Europe help explain this pattern. When certain pesticides are no longer approved for use within the EU, permissible residue limits are reviewed and often lowered, and these standards apply to imports as well . Produce grown using those substances, even if permitted in countries like India, therefore faces greater scrutiny at EU borders. These standards are beginning to influence other markets. In February 2026, APEDA advised exporters to exercise caution in shipments to Lebanon after the country moved to align its residue limits more closely with EU norms. Regulatory benchmarks set in one market are increasingly shaping access in others, expanding the compliance challenge beyond Europe. Production Challenges and Technology Gaps India has responded through export-side controls. Requirements such as Certificates of Inspection by EIC or EIA for rice exports to the EU and certain European markets play an important role in pre-shipment verification. These measures help manage risks at the border but do not address the underlying compliance challenge. The production side is equally central. Indian farmers continue to rely significantly on crop protection molecules introduced three to four decades ago. Government estimates place annual crop losses from pests and diseases in the range of 10–35%, with economic losses exceeding Rs 2 lakh crore. Export markets are setting tighter residue thresholds at the same time. Managing both effectively requires access to crop protection solutions that are not only effective against evolving pest pressures but are also compatible with stricter global residue expectations. The pace at which newer crop protection technologies reach Indian farms has been uneven. Developing a new molecule is expensive and time-consuming, and companies must generate local data on safety, bio-efficacy, residues and environmental impact before introduction. In the absence of a clear and time-bound domestic framework that protects this data, companies tend to prioritise markets where regulatory pathways offer greater certainty. This delays the availability of newer technologies in India and contributes to continued reliance on older chemistries, even as export markets tighten residue expectations. The draft Pesticides Management Bill addresses several long-standing issues such as action against spurious products, digitised processes and time-bound approvals. However, it does not set out a clear approach for the treatment of regulatory data generated for product approvals. This creates a gap in how the system supports the timely introduction of newer crop protection technologies. A limited and clearly defined approach to Protection of Regulatory Data can help address this gap. In simple terms, it allows the company that generates the required safety and efficacy data for a new product a defined period before that data can be relied upon by others. This enables earlier introduction of newer crop protection technologies, with a direct bearing on how quickly farmers can align practices with evolving residue standards in export markets, while maintaining a balance between innovation, competition and affordability. As the Bill is taken up for consideration, incorporating such an approach within the regulatory framework would play an important role in strengthening both farm outcomes and export readiness. This issue has been examined in India’s own policy discussions. The Satwant Reddy Committee in 2007 recommended a limited form of protection for agrochemical data, and the Committee on Doubling Farmers’ Income also suggested a calibrated protection approach for introducing new molecules. The Parliamentary Standing Committee that reviewed the 2020 Bill considered these options alongside the need to support domestic generics and affordability. What remains unresolved is how quickly newer crop protection technologies can reach Indian farms under the current framework. That question is now immediate. Export markets are facing disruption due to ongoing conflicts, while high-value markets such as the EU are tightening residue expectations even as farmers continue to rely on older chemistries. Therefore, faster access to newer solutions can help reduce some of these gaps, improve resistance management, and support compliance with evolving standards in key markets. As Parliament takes up the new pesticide law, the design of the framework will determine how effectively Indian agriculture can respond to changing pest pressures while remaining aligned with increasingly demanding global markets, where compliance is becoming a precondition for access. (Ankur Aggarwal, Chairman of CropLife India and Executive Chairman & Managing Director of Crystal Crop Protection Ltd.) Views are personal, and do not represent the stand of this publication. Title: OPINION | India strengthens border infrastructure as China builds strategic advantage- Moneycontrol.com
Description: India is expanding border infrastructure to match China. Rising tensions and past gaps show the need for faster development to improve defence and strengthen its position
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: While the rules-based international order continues to be strained across the globe amid intensifying conflicts, sovereignty remains a key reason for contestation. In this context, the India–China relationship is one that cannot be missed. While diplomatic engagements with Beijing continue, in line with the necessity of adhering to the rules-based international order, India is also reshaping the strategic landscape along the Line of Actual Control (LAC) through enhanced border management. India’s Ministry of Home Affairs recently confirmed that the Indo-Tibetan Border Police (ITBP) has set up 29 new border outposts along the India–China border since 2020, bringing the total to 209 between 2022 and 2024. These posts span altitudes from 9,000 feet to 18,750 feet, stretching from the Karakoram Pass in Ladakh to Jachep La in Arunachal Pradesh. The announcement drew limited headlines, but it speaks volumes about the enduring suspicions in India–China relations, beneath the diplomatic niceties exchanged since October 2024. Both governments, in India and China, have celebrated what they call a "reset" - the restoration of patrolling rights at Depsang and Demchok , ministers exchanging visits, and the resumption of direct flights. Yet on the ground, both sides have been engaged in something far more consequential than dialogue: an infrastructure build up quietly reshaping the Himalayan frontier since 2017. To understand why India's ITBP expansion matters, one must first reckon with the scale of what China has already built. While the majority of discourse centres on the 1950s, a renewed focus has emerged, especially post-Doklam. Beijing launched an accelerated infrastructure-building drive along its side of the LAC immediately after the 2017 Doklam stand-off. The extent, as underemphasised, is staggering. Between 2018 and 2022 , China constructed 624 “xiaokang” (well-off) villages in Tibet, with 72% concentrated opposite India's northeastern states, and a full 90% of those facing Arunachal Pradesh. These are not simply civilian settlements. Many feature helipads, walled barracks-style complexes, radar installations and what Indian Army officers have carefully described as "dual-use facilities." The village-building was accompanied by an extraordinary expansion of hard military infrastructure. The Lhasa-Nyingchi Railway, which commenced operations in June 2021 , brought a functioning railhead to within 30 kilometres of the Arunachal Pradesh border. This distance a mechanised column can cover in under an hour. A permanent bridge across Pangong Tso, built in 2022 and capable of carrying armoured columns, cut deployment time from 12 hours to four. Since 2017, China has built or substantially upgraded 37 airports and heliports in Tibet and Xinjiang. New strategic highways. This includes the G-695, running parallel to the LAC from near Arunachal Pradesh to the Karakoram, each of which has been designated and is under construction. In July 2025, China broke ground on the world's largest hydroelectric dam across the Brahmaputra, a project that gives Beijing an entirely new category of leverage over downstream countries. China's 15th Five-Year Plan, launched this year, doubles down on all of this, proposing further highway construction in Xinjiang to improve military mobility in the western sector – including a new 394-km route running parallel to the strategic Aksai Chin road. The infrastructure buildup on the Indian side needs to be contextualised against this backdrop. It arrived late, and the catch-up required accelerated development in both extent and utility. The strategic all-weather Daulat Beg Oldi road, connecting the world's highest airstrip to the rest of Ladakh, was completed only in 2020. The Arunachal Frontier Highway, running within 20 kilometres of the LAC, is slated to be operational by 2027. In December 2025, 125 new border infrastructure projects were inaugurated, including 93 bridges and 28 roads worth INR 5000 crore. This was BRO’s largest ever single-day launch. The Mudh-Nyoma Air Force Station in Ladakh, perched at 13,700 feet and just 30 kilometres from the LAC, became operational in 2025 , years after India began seriously upgrading it from a simple landing strip into a fighter-capable airbase. The Indian Air Force is now reportedly building at least six additional airbases on its side of the border, within 25 to 150 kilometres of the LAC. On the civilian side, the Vibrant Villages Programme, launched in 2022 to mirror China's xiaokang strategy, aims to develop 663 border villages with modern amenities and connectivity, thus reversing the depopulation of frontier areas that had left stretches of India's border effectively unmanned and unmonitored. The ITBP, meanwhile, has announced 41 more forward bases along the LAC in the near future, alongside new Shaurya commando platoons training jointly with the Army in high-altitude warfare and drone operations. The 29 new ITBP outposts confirmed this week are the latest increment in this broader pattern, and are essential forelementary deterrence, long overdue. The 2020 Galwan clashes were partly made possible because China's People’s Liberation Amry (PLA) had quietly acquired, over years of infrastructure investment, the ability to rapidly induct troops, armour, and supplies into positions along the LAC that India could not match in speed or scale. Infrastructure, in the Himalayan context, is not a supporting element of military power. It is military power. The October 2024 border agreement was welcome, and the diplomatic thaw that followed it is real. But both sides have continued building throughout the negotiations, and both will continue building after them. China was India's second-largest trading partner in FY 2024–25, yet India's trade deficit has ballooned to over USD 99 billion. The Brahmaputra dam is already under construction. The Dalai Lama’s succession looms as a flashpoint. It was described by interlocutors in Beijing as a “live issue.” The China-Pakistan axis shows no signs of weakening, and China has extended its claim of "core interest" to cover Arunachal Pradesh , according to the US Congress's latest annual China military report. New ITBP outposts, new airbases, new roads. This will go on. After decades of neglect, it simply has to. The structural drivers of the India-China rivalry: an unresolved border, an asymmetric trade relationship, and Beijing's unwillingness to treat New Delhi as a peer, remain entirely intact. For India, border infrastructure is not a statement of hostile intent. It is the minimum condition for sitting at a table where the other side arrived, and was built decades earlier. As stated by India’s External Affairs Minister, Dr. S. Jaishankar, in January this year, Arunachal Pradesh is and always will remain a part of India, and no action by any country can change the reality. The bolstering of Indian defence by the ITBP near the borders is a reflection of the reality, and needs to be read in the context. (Tejusvi Shukla (Doctoral Candidate, OP Jindal Global University and Prof. (Dr.) Sriparna Pathak, OP Jindal Global University.) Views are personal, and do not represent the stand of this publication. Title: Kerala Assembly Election: Can Congress breach Left bastion as BJP expands footprint?- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Kerala’s 2026 assembly poll is shaping up as a tight, wave-less contest, with Congress banking on voter fatigue, LDF leaning on governance, and BJP seeking to convert vote share gains into seats.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Since the 2008 delimitation, Kerala’s electoral trajectory has largely favoured CPI(M)-led Left Democratic Front (LDF), barring the narrow United Democratic Front (UDF) victory in 2011. Congress-led UDF last came to power in 2011, winning 72 seats-just above the majority mark of 71 in the 140-member assembly. LDF returned strongly in 2016 with 91 seats and, in a historic break from Kerala’s four-decade pattern of alternating governments, retained power in 2021 with 99 seats under Chief Minister Pinarayi Vijayan. The ruling front is now seeking a rare third consecutive term. At the constituency level, shifts triggered by delimitation continue to shape outcomes. Take Mavelikara assembly segment in Alappuzha: once a Congress stronghold with four consecutive wins between 1991 and 2006, it became a Scheduled Caste-reserved seat after delimitation in 2011 (Kerala’s delimitation order was issued in 2008 and first implemented in the 2011 assembly elections). Since then, CPI(M) has won it three times, underlining how structural changes have altered electoral arithmetic. Also, read : Kerala Elections 2026 Live: Nearly 50% voter turnout till 1 pm; Congress MP Shashi Tharoor says 'BJP not a factor' More broadly, LDF is seen to retain a solid base, with around 50 constituencies staying with it across 2011, 2016 and 2021 elections. In contrast, UDF is estimated to have about 25 relatively secure seats, implying a steep climb to reach the halfway mark. CPI(M) has fielded 56 sitting MLAs, relying on incumbency, while the ECI’s March 15 announcement of April 9 polling left just 25 days for campaigning-one of Kerala’s shortest-making it difficult for UDF and BJP-led NDA candidates unfamiliar with the constituencies. While UDF continues to rely on traditional bastions such as Congress strongholds in Ernakulam and Kottayam, as well as IUML-influenced regions including parts of north Kerala such as Malappuram, battle is likely to hinge on its performance outside these pockets. With no clear statewide swing, 2026 Kerala assembly election is likely to be decided by narrow margins and constituency-level dynamics. LDF is betting on continuity and governance, UDF on voter fatigue and revival, and BJP on expanding its footprint. In a finely balanced contest, even small shifts in vote share could have outsized consequences. Voting began at 7am across Kerala’s 140 constituencies for 883 candidates with over 2.7 crore voters. By 3pm, nearly 63 percent of the electorate had cast their votes. The 2026 assembly election is widely seen as a wave-less contest, with no single dominant narrative shaping voter sentiment. Instead, a combination of welfare delivery, local issues, community dynamics and pockets of anti-incumbency is expected to determine outcomes. While Kerala remains structurally bipolar, triangular contests are emerging in several constituencies with the growing presence of Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), adding a new layer of complexity and tightening margins. Congress eyes comeback amid national setbacks For Congress, Kerala is one of the few realistic opportunities to regain political ground nationally after a series of electoral setbacks in states such as Haryana, Maharashtra, Bihar and Delhi. In the state, party is banking on voter fatigue against incumbent government, raising issues such as Sabarimala gold theft case and corruption, while also rolling out welfare guarantees on the lines of its campaigns in Karnataka and Telangana. However, UDF’s campaign has been weighed down by misappropriation of funds meant for the Wayanad landslide disaster housing project, delays in candidate selection, factional differences and discontent over the induction of defectors. Differences among senior leaders, including former state party chief K Sudhakaran, have surfaced intermittently, affecting organisational cohesion at a crucial juncture. LDF is pitching its governance record and welfare delivery to counter anti-incumbency. The alliance is highlighting infrastructure push through Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board (KIIFB) funds and continuity in social welfare programmes as major achievements. At the same time, it faces criticism over fiscal stress, unemployment and inflation-issues. BJP looks to convert vote share into seats Meanwhile, BJP is aiming to translate its steady rise in vote share into tangible seat gains. Its vote share in Kerala assembly elections has increased from about 4.75 percent in 2006 to over 11 percent in 2021, though this has not yet translated into seats. The only time BJP won a seat in the Kerala assembly was in 2016, when O Rajagopal won from Nemom. The party is focusing on select constituencies such as Palakkad, Nemom and Manjeshwaram. Its victory in Thrissur in 2024 Lok Sabha election has provided momentum but converting that into assembly seats remains a challenge. Kerala’s electoral dynamics continue to be shaped by community alignments. UDF draws strength from Muslim and Christian voters, while LDF retains a strong base among Ezhavas, Dalits and sections of the working class. However, subtle shifts within these blocs could prove decisive in a close contest, as BJP seeks to woo both Christian and Ezhava voters. Gulf diaspora continues to influence Kerala elections. With an estimated 30-35 lakh Malayalees in GCC countries, many were hesitant to travel due to regional tensions and the absence of the usual special flights organised by political parties, potentially lowering voter turnout in northern Kerala.
