Title: India open to global capital, strengthening market framework: SEBI chief Tuhin Kanta Pandey- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The session jointly organized by the Confederation of Indian Industry and the Consulate General of India, San Francisco, brought together stakeholders from the Silicon Valley venture capital ecosystem to explore investment opportunities and discuss the evolving India–US economic partnership.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: India continues to position itself as a compelling destination for global capital, backed by strong macroeconomic fundamentals, a growing investor base, and a reform-driven policy landscape, said Tuhin Kanta Pandey, Chairman of Securities and Exchange Board of India, during a session with global investors and venture capital leaders in San Francisco. According to a release, the session, jointly organized by the Confederation of Indian Industry and the Consulate General of India, San Francisco, brought together stakeholders from the Silicon Valley venture capital ecosystem to explore investment opportunities and discuss the evolving India–US economic partnership. Addressing participants, Pandey reaffirmed SEBI’s commitment to a transparent, consultative, and technology-driven regulatory framework. He emphasized India’s resilience amid global economic volatility and highlighted sustained macroeconomic strength. “India remains open and welcoming to global capital. SEBI’s approach is risk-based and facilitative, with a strong focus on simplifying access for foreign investors, strengthening market integrity, and ensuring long-term stability of our capital markets,” he said. Pandey pointed to key reforms, including simplification of Foreign Portfolio Investor (FPI) registration and Re-KYC processes, expanded use of digital platforms, and the steady growth of IPO activity and alternative investment funds. He also highlighted the increasing role of domestic investors in enhancing market depth and resilience. Controlled inflation, robust foreign exchange reserves, and stable external accounts, he noted, continue to underpin India’s economic strength. K Srikar Reddy, Consul General of India in San Francisco, said India’s growth continues to be driven by reforms, a strong startup ecosystem, and expanding digital public infrastructure. “India is among the fastest-growing major economies. Strengthening partnerships with global investors, especially from the United States, will be important as India works towards becoming a developed nation by 2047,” he said. He also noted that India–US trade has crossed USD 240 billion, with both sides aiming to take it to USD 500 billion by 2030. R Mukundan, President Designate of CII and Managing Director and CEO of Tata Chemicals, said collaboration between government, industry, and global investors will be key to sustaining growth. “Global partnerships, particularly with the US, are important for scaling innovation, strengthening manufacturing, and creating long-term value,” he said. Participants also share feedback on easing regulatory processes, improving clarity in cross-border investment rules, and increasing access to growth-stage capital, especially for deep-tech companies. They also called for faster digital processes and stronger exit options, including mergers and acquisitions, along with greater focus on sustainability and ESG. Title: FPIs dump Rs 48,213 crore in April; 2026 outflows near Rs 1.8 lakh crore- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The latest exodus follows a record outflow of Rs 1.17 lakh crore in March, the worst monthly withdrawal on record, marking a sharp reversal from February, when FPIs had infused Rs 22,615 crore, the highest monthly inflow in 17 months.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) continued their heavy selling in Indian equities, pulling out Rs 48,213 crore (USD 5.14 billion) in the first 10 days of April, as rising geopolitical tensions and global macroeconomic uncertainty dampened risk appetite. The latest exodus follows a record outflow of Rs 1.17 lakh crore in March, the worst monthly withdrawal on record, marking a sharp reversal from February, when FPIs had infused Rs 22,615 crore, the highest monthly inflow in 17 months. With the April sell-off, total FPI outflows in 2026 have surged to around Rs 1.8 lakh crore, according to data from the National Securities Depository Ltd (NSDL). In April alone, the withdrawals have been concentrated in the cash market. Market experts say the sustained selling pressure is being driven by a mix of global headwinds and elevated geopolitical risks. Himanshu Srivastava, Principal - Manager Research at Morningstar Investment Research India, said risk aversion has intensified due to escalating tensions in West Asia, which have pushed up crude oil prices and revived global inflation concerns. VK Vijayakumar, Chief Investment Strategist at Geojit Investments, pointed to the broader fallout of the energy crisis linked to the West Asia conflict. He said concerns over its spillover impact on the Indian economy, along with continued depreciation of the rupee, have kept foreign investors in a “sell mode”. He also noted that alternative markets such as South Korea and Taiwan are currently drawing more FPI interest due to their stronger earnings growth outlook, compared to relatively modest expectations for India in FY27. Interestingly, even the recent US-Iran ceasefire failed to reverse the trend. Vaqarjaved Khan, Senior Fundamental Analyst at Angel One, said FPIs used the relief rally as an opportunity to exit. “FPIs used the relief rally as a liquidity window to exit further,” he noted. Khan added that a turnaround in flows would depend on key triggers, including a credible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, stabilisation in the rupee, and a positive surprise from India’s Q4 earnings season. “Flows can reverse quickly, but only if macro conditions begin to support the shift,” he said. Title: Bill Ackman's Pershing Square proposes $64 billion merger deal with Universal Music Group- Moneycontrol.com
Description: UMG's shares have lost almost a third of their value since its IPO and currently trade at a long-term multiple of 21.8 times earnings, compared with Spotify's 40 times, according to LSEG data
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Bill Ackman's Pershing Square offered to buy Universal Music Group on Tuesday in a $64 billion deal via its acquisition vehicle that would move the world's biggest music label's listing to New York in a bid to revive its value. Pershing Square's cash-and-shares offer values Universal Music at around 30.40 euros per share - a 78% premium to the last closing price of 17.10 euros - making the deal worth 55.75 billion euros ($64.31 billion), according to Reuters calculations. Universal Music Group - the company behind international superstars including Taylor Swift, Billie Eilish and Kendrick Lamar - declined a Reuters request for comment on the proposed deal. "The offer is non-binding and might well fail but we think at a minimum it has the merit of raising valid questions and making the case for dramatic changes," said analysts at ING, adding that they expected investors to carefully consider the proposal. Shares in the entertainment company, which is listed in Amsterdam, were up 10% at 1355 on Tuesday, while top shareholder Bollore Group climbed 5%. Shares in Vivendi, UMG's second-biggest shareholder, were up over 10%. HOPES U.S. LISTING WILL BOOST UMG AS INDUSTRY FACES UPHEAVAL Pershing bought a 10% stake in UMG from Vivendi ahead of its 2021 Amsterdam IPO and has since repeatedly pressed for a New York listing, arguing it would boost UMG's share price and liquidity. It cut its stake to 7.48% last year to trigger a mechanism for the U.S. move. But in March, UMG said market conditions had forced it to delay its plan for a U.S. listing. Pershing currently has a 4.7% stake, making it UMG's fourth-biggest shareholder. In a letter to UMG directors, Ackman said its management had done an "excellent" job of running a strong business and strategic execution. But he blamed uncertainty over the 18% stake held by Bollore Group, the U.S. listing delay and underutilisation of its balance sheet, among other things, for its low share price. UMG's shares have lost almost a third of their value since its IPO and currently trade at a long-term multiple of 21.8 times earnings, compared with Spotify's 40 times, according to LSEG data. Even as global music revenues grow year after year, UMG and other major labels like Sony and Warner Music are scrambling to stay competitive as streaming services from Spotify, Amazon, Apple, and Deezer take an ever greater share. They are now also contending with disruptions brought on by the expansion of artificial intelligence - from copyright disputes to the advent of song-generating AI tools - that threaten to upend how music is created, consumed and monetised. One survey last year found a staggering 97% of listeners cannot distinguish between AI-generated and human-composed songs. UNDER DEAL, MICHAEL OVITZ WOULD COME IN AS BOARD CHAIR Under Tuesday's proposal, Pershing's SPARC Holdings would merge with UMG and the new entity would become a Nevada corporation listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Renowned Hollywood talent agent and former Walt Disney Company president Michael Ovitz would join the board as chairman with two Pershing representatives also taking seats, Ackman's letter said. The deal could prompt UMG management to leave, the ING analysts said, given that they had wanted a free hand in growing in emerging markets through M&A deals targeting 1 billion euros a year over the next few years. "This seems a rather direct rebuttal of this strategy," they said. Pershing Square said that under the transaction UMG shareholders would receive a total of 9.4 billion euros in cash and 0.77 shares in the new company for every share held in UMG. The cash portion of the new proposed deal would be funded by Pershing from its SPARC's rights holders, debt, and net proceeds from the company's stake in Spotify, it said. The transaction would be subject to approval by the UMG and SPARC boards, a two-thirds vote in favour by UMG shareholders in attendance at a meeting and required regulatory approvals, it said, and is expected to close by the end of the year. ACKMAN NEEDS 'FULL-ON CHARM OFFENSIVE', ANALYST SAYS The move underscores Ackman's ambition for large deals, said Dan Coatsworth, head of markets at AJ Bell, who said the Pershing founder had long admired Warren Buffett's style of finding good companies going cheap and buying them outright. But he added Ackman would need a "full-on charm offensive" to win over major shareholders like China's Tencent and 74-year-old French billionaire Vincent Bollore, who drove UMG's Amsterdam listing, one of the largest IPOs in European history at the time. Bollore Group owns 18.5% of UMG but has additional exposure through Vivendi, which it partly owns and holds 13.4% of UMG. Through those stakes Bollore commands over 80% of Universal's voting rights, according to Universal's investor relations page. Bollore Group did not respond to a request for comment, while Vivendi declined to comment on the proposal. Tencent Holdings, UMG's third-biggest shareholder, did not respond to a request for comment. Title: US satellite 'blackouts' cast a shadow on Indian spacetech players with American ties- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Recent US satellite curbs raise fresh questions on control and autonomy for Indian spacetech firms abroad
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: When San Francisco-headquartered satellite imaging firm Planet Labs, on the directions of the US government, restricted access to visuals of Iran and the wider conflict-hit West Asia last week, it was seen as a tactical move in a volatile theatre. For India's emerging spacetech ecosystem, and especially those with ties to the US and other countries, it has thrown up a bigger question: who controls space-based data and infrastructure? On April 4, Planet Labs announced an indefinite restriction on high-resolution imagery of Iran and West Asia, backdated to March 9, on the request of the Trump administration. Space tech startups such as Pixxel, Digantara and Bellatrix Aerospace have over the past few years set up subsidiaries or operations in the US to tap into deeper pools of capital, access customers, and integrate into global supply chains. That global footprint, however, also places them within overlapping regulatory and geopolitical spheres of influence. Executives, lawyers and policy experts say the answer is neither simple nor entirely commercial. Chaitanya Giri, fellow at the Centre for Security, Strategy and Technology at Observer Research Foundation, points to a deeper structural alignment between US private space firms and national security priorities. "American companies, especially those embedded in defence-linked ecosystems, operate within a framework of implicit commitments. When geopolitical situations escalate, compliance is not optional – it is built into how these firms function," he said, referring to Washington’s long-standing strategic coordination with Israel. This is not new. During the Kargil War, the US famously denied India access to high-resolution satellite imagery. What has changed is the scale of private participation — and the degree to which startups are now plugged into global capital and markets. For startups like Digantara, the response has been architectural. "Whether the linkage is capital, customers, or incorporation, the risk is when decision-making and data flows are centralised within one jurisdiction," Digantara Industries founder and CEO Anirudh Sharma said. "In our case, that risk is structurally mitigated. Our US and India entities operate independently under distinct regulatory frameworks with differentiated mandates," he told Moneycontrol. Digantara's model reflects a shift that is underway in the industry — moving away from centralised global operations towards jurisdiction-specific structures. "Our IP is designed and owned in India. Entities in other markets operate on the basis of licences derived from that core, building local capabilities on top of it," he said. "This creates a locally rooted offering in each market rather than a single centralised product pushed across geographies." Digantara’s US entity, for instance, focuses on capabilities aligned with that market, including missile tracking and early-warning systems, while its India operations remain centred on space domain awareness. Industry executives say such "ring-fencing" is fast becoming the norm rather than the exception. "It’s about financial pragmatism," said an industry executive, requesting anonymity. "You go where the capital is but you structure yourself so that no single geography can dictate your entire operation." Legal experts say this shift towards multi-jurisdictional structuring is no longer optional; it is becoming foundational to how spacetech firms operate globally. Spacetech firms must now embed geopolitical risk into their business models, Ashok GV, Partner at Allied Law Practices, said. "In this evolving multipolar landscape, corporations must incorporate geopolitical due diligence into their core business planning. This entails carefully structuring networks of subsidiaries and holding entities across jurisdictions in ways that both ensure compliance with export control regimes and reduce operational frictions associated with cross-border licensing," Ashok said. "The strategic localisation of research, development and intellectual property generation can enable firms to operate more autonomously within different regulatory environments, while still contributing meaningfully to host state priorities," he said. Citing John Chipman’s essay in Harvard Business Review on corporate foreign policy, Ashok said, “Emerging space companies such as Digantara and Pixxel would benefit from articulating a ‘corporate foreign policy’ that enables each entity within their corporate structure to generate localised value, minimise conflicts of interest, and navigate geopolitical sensitivities responsibly.” Ashok suggested shifting domiciles to neutral jurisdictions such as Singapore or Switzerland, distributing IP across countries and even setting up independent control centres to ring-fence operations. At a legal level, the country in which a satellite is authorised is expected to exercise primary control but in practice, it is only a part of the story. "Authorisation (of the satellite) sets the legal baseline but in practice, multiple factors determine satellite operations," Sharma said. "Regulations themselves are not static, they shift with geopolitics. Sanctions, export restrictions, and conflict-driven bans can alter what operators can and cannot do overnight." This creates a layered system of influence involving export controls, component dependencies and capital flows. Another startup executive requesting anonymity said US investment does not automatically translate into control but acknowledged the presence of "pressure points". “If US money is building that infrastructure, there will inevitably be levers — whether through compliance requirements, supply chains or access to markets," the person added . A senior industry expert framed it more starkly. "Capital itself becomes leverage. There are many more levers beyond ownership. If you fall out of favour, you drop in the pecking order," the executive said. This underlying dynamic is also shaped by Washington’s evolving view of space as strategic infrastructure. As highlighted in an ORF analysis on the “recalibration” of STEM and defence priorities in the US, the American war department is increasingly aligning private innovation ecosystems with national security objectives — blurring the line between commercial and sovereign capabilities. This helps explain why actions like the Planet Labs blackout are less ad hoc and more systemic. India is attempting a delicate balancing act. Initiatives by IN-SPACe (Earth observation PPP) and government-backed funding mechanisms signal an intent to build domestic capacity and reduce external dependencies. But gaps remain. "There is still a shortage of patient capital for space infrastructure in India,” an executive said. “Government schemes are largely focused on R&D. The heavy lifting — manufacturing, deployment — still needs deeper pockets.” This tension — between access to global capital and the need for strategic autonomy — is unlikely to disappear. For Digantara, the two are not mutually exclusive. "The spacetech market is at an inflection point, and firms that build sovereign, locally-rooted infrastructure from the start are better positioned than those retrofitting it later," Sharma said. As more Indian startups expand into the US and other markets, the Planet Labs episode may well become a reference point — not just for risk but for how companies design themselves in a more fragmented, multipolar space economy.
Keywords: auto, defence, geopolitics, ipo, policy, sanction, supply chain, war

Title: FIIs turn net buyers for first time since Feb 25, at Rs 672 crore; DIIs net buy Rs 410 crore on April 10- Moneycontrol.com
Description: During the trading session, DIIs picked up shares worth Rs 15,982 crore and sold shares worth Rs 15,572 crore. FIIs on the other hand, purchased shares worth Rs 18,304 crore and offloaded shares worth Rs 17,632 crore.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Foreign investors (FIIs/FPIs) turned net buyers for the first time since February 25, to the tune of Rs 672.09 crore worth of Indian equities on April 10. At the same time, domestic institutional investors (DIIs) net bought shares worth Rs 410.05 crore, according to provisional exchange data. During the trading session on Friday, DIIs picked up shares worth Rs 15,982 crore and sold shares worth Rs 15,572 crore. FIIs on the other hand, purchased shares worth Rs 18,304 crore and offloaded shares worth Rs 17,632 crore. For the year so far, FIIs have been net sellers of shares worth Rs 2.12 lakh crore, while DIIs have net bought shares worth Rs 2.77 lakh crore. The buying from both sides - domestic as well as foreign investors - on Friday also supported the market rally that already driven by the US-Israel-Iran ceasefire and fall in oil prices to below $100 a barrel level. At close, the Sensex surged 919 points or 1.2 percent to 77,550, and the Nifty soared 276 points or 1.16 percent to 24,051. Nifty Midcap and Smallcap indices also participated in the rally, rising 1.52 percent and 1.65 percent, respectively. "Indian equities were supported by positive global cues and optimism surrounding geopolitical de-escalation efforts in West Asia. Buying interest was broad based, with mid cap and small cap stocks outperforming large caps," Vinod Nair, Head of Research at Geojit Investments said. All sectors, barring IT, participated in the market uptrend with auto, banking & financials and realty leading the charge with 2-3 percent rally supported by comfort on valuations. IT stocks underperformed following TCS quarterly numbers, and as concerns over AI related disruptions prompted profit booking following the recent rally in the sector. Looking ahead, while markets closely track the outcome of US-Iran peace talks this weekend, valuations and a stable medium term earnings trajectory will guide the markets direction, Vinod Nair said. The benchmark Nifty 50 gained 5.89 percent this week, the biggest weekly rally since February 2021, while FII selling intensity moderated from Wednesday onwards, may be signalling a potential easing in foreign outflow pressure. "While the bounce is positive, we expect the market to remain range-bound in the near term," Vikram Kasat, Head Advisory at PL Capital said. Sustained upward momentum will depend on global stability, FII flows, and upcoming corporate earnings, with investors likely to stay selective and favour quality stocks, he believes. Asian Paints, Eicher Motors, Bajaj Auto, ICICI Bank, and Mahindra and Mahindra were among top gainers on the Nifty 50, while losers were Coal India, Sun Pharmaceutical Industries, Infosys, Tata Consultancy Services, and Tech Mahindra. Title: Europe energy traders brace for 21-hour day as volatility surges- Moneycontrol.com
Description: With traders glued to their screens, preparations for the new 21-hour trading day have lagged. Some firms are taking a wait-and-see approach on whether to hire more staff or reorganize their desks.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: European gas and power markets, once a niche corner of global energy trading, are in for a major change next week: Hours will more than double to 21 from 10, ending the narrow daytime window that has been the norm for years. The permanent expansion, announced several months ago, comes at a particularly volatile time as war in the Middle East drives sharp price swings. Even after the recent ceasefire announcement, contracts for European gas remain almost 40% above pre-conflict levels. With traders glued to their screens, preparations for the new 21-hour trading day have lagged. Some firms are taking a wait-and-see approach on whether to hire more staff or reorganize their desks. Yet despite the turbulence, many energy traders say the change is a logical next step for a market transformed by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Europe’s break from a single dominant energy supplier pushed it into the global liquefied national gas market, exposing prices to disruptions far beyond the region. At the same time, the growth of renewables has added further swings in power generation, feeding through to gas demand. These developments have made European gas and power more attractive to global traders. What was once a fundamentals-driven market, focused on weather, storage and pipeline flows, now features more complex strategies, including cross-market hedging between Europe’s gas benchmark and US Henry Hub. Options activity has picked up from near non-existent levels, while spread trading has grown amid persistent supply risks and hedge funds have driven volumes higher. To many, aligning trading hours with US and Asian benchmarks widens investor access and better reflects a market with expanding reach and relevance. The market will open at 5:50 a.m. Singapore time on Monday, making it the first major energy contract to begin the trading week. The new 21-hour day is “an advantage for global players,” said Marco Saalfrank, head of continental Europe merchant trading at Swiss-based Axpo Holding AG. “If something happens in the market that affects prices, they can react immediately.” For traders on the ground in Europe, the longer window is set to reshape how they work and live. Until now, the routine has been relatively structured: Traders log on before markets open at 8 a.m. in Amsterdam, assess fundamentals such as flows and storage, scan weather and geopolitical developments, and take positions at roughly the same time. When the hours expand, that rhythm will fragment across time zones, with participants dipping in and out of the market much like they do in oil. Whether that’s a net positive depends on whom you ask. In interviews with European traders, the mood was mixed, with concerns that a 21-hour market could erode a work-life balance already rare in the industry. A Swiss trading desk head said he worries about missing his children’s bedtime as market-moving headlines from US President Donald Trump on Iran often land after 6 p.m. in Europe. A London-based trader said unreliable train Wi-Fi could make the evening commute more stressful, while a utility trader joked he would expect a pay rise if longer hours become the norm. Some plan to set alarms overnight to monitor markets. Operational questions are also mounting. A Singapore-based LNG trader said expanded hours will require far more cross-time-zone coordination, complicating decision-making. Others question whether liquidity will keep pace or simply spread volatility over a longer trading day. The changes don’t have “obvious benefits to localized trading demographics,” said Tim Partridge, head of trading and risk management at LG Energy Group in the UK. One London-based trader at a small fund said his team is largely Europe-based, leaving him unable to rely on handovers to US or Asian desks. Across the Atlantic, the reaction has been more positive. A trader at a cross-commodity hedge fund in Chicago said the move is a boon for paper traders, allowing algorithms to train during periods when liquidity is thinner. There is also a view that longer trading hours could help smooth some of the sharp price swings seen in recent weeks. Compared with Europe, US power markets have long traded across a much wider window. A Miami-based portfolio manager, who asked not to be identified, trades oil and US natural gas alongside European energy futures and said punishing hours are part of the job. He is used to 14-hour shifts and often works from home on Sundays. For him, the change is overdue: If oil traders can operate in a global, near round-the-clock market, European gas and power traders should be able to as well. Title: A decade on UPI’s made India a payment leader. Here’s what we need now- Moneycontrol.com
Description: UPI's interoperability with the digital rupee signals something larger, it’s evolving from a payments network into an interface layer for India's monetary infrastructure
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Ten years ago, India attempted something no large economy had tried - a single real-time interoperable payments network, working across hundreds of banks and every smartphone that could run an app. What began as an infrastructure experiment in 2016 has become how 1.4 billion people transact. UPI now accounts for 85% of all digital payments in India, and nearly half of all real-time payments on the planet. That achievement deserves celebration. But if all we do at the ten-year mark is admire the rail, we miss what matters most about the next ten – what is happening inside the banks that connect to it. Can they do more than move money? NPCI Built a Platform. Most Banks Plugged in a Pipe. For most banks, UPI is a high-performance payment pipe. Fast, reliable and massive in scale - but still a pipe where money goes in and money comes out. UPI was designed for far more: credit, recurring mandates, merchant services, cross-border flows. The building blocks exist. But somewhere between the NPCI spec and the bank’s backend, ambition has implicitly narrowed into basic payment processing. The constraint is hardly intent. Banks want to do more with UPI. Their internal systems were built for a different era and stretching them to match what UPI now makes possible is harder than it looks from the outside. To be clear, this is not about bad decisions. India's bank cores were state-of-the-art when deployed - built for branch-led banking, batch processing, end-of-day reconciliation. They have served hundreds of millions of customers reliably, and they continue to. But UPI demands something different. When a payment hits a bank, the window to act on it - offer credit, flag fraud, personalize an experience - lasts milliseconds. Most bank systems were designed to think overnight whereas the rail has moved to a new speed. The systems behind it, largely, have not. RBI cleared it and NPCI built the plumbing. The demand is obvious: over 420 million people use UPI and only 110 million have credit cards. Hundreds of millions of creditworthy Indians remain outside formal credit. What makes Credit Line on UPI powerful is contextual credit: a merchant getting working capital based on the settlement patterns, or a consumer offered a credit option based on what they are buying. Credit at the right moment, not days after an application winds through a processing cycle. For that to work, eligibility assessment, limit management, and risk decisioning - all need to happen within the transaction – not after it. This capability can be built as a new layer without ripping out the existing core systems. Banks that invest in building this layer will unlock a credit distribution model that no other payments rail in the world currently enables at this scale. Insurance premiums, SIP investments, loan EMIs - the entire recurring payments economy could run on UPI Autopay. It has the bones to become India's default subscription infrastructure. But a mandate is not a one-time instruction. It is a relationship that changes - amounts shift, customers pause, plans get modified, notifications must go out on time. Managing that lifecycle at scale requires responsiveness that many bank backends were not designed for. The solution, once again, does not require wholesale replacement - it requires a focused, purpose-built capability sitting alongside existing systems. Identity: What Makes Everything Else Work Credit decisions, personalised experiences, mandate management - none of it works without knowing with certainty who is transacting. UPI's move toward biometric authentication is the right direction and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) adds weight: identity is no longer just compliance, it is a trust advantage. Banks that get identity right earn the right to do far more than process payments. Nobody should be ripping out core banking systems that hold decades of customer data, lending history, and compliance infrastructure. That is neither practical nor wise. What banks need is a modern capability layer alongside these systems. Something that handles the real-time, decision-heavy work that UPI's new use cases demand, while the core continues doing what it does well i.e. maintaining the ledger, managing risk, and staying compliant. The difference between a bank that processes a UPI payment and one that can act on it - offer a product, make a lending decision, or personalize an experience - will define market share over the next decade. India built something extraordinary, and the world has taken notice. Multiple countries are studying UPI or building versions of their own. Internationalization of UPI will define the next phase. UPI is already live in several markets and cross-border corridors are expanding but settling these transactions in real-time demands capabilities most banks do not yet have: multi-currency, multi-jurisdiction compliance, correspondent banking, all at payment speed. And UPI's interoperability with the digital rupee signals something larger - UPI is evolving from a payments network into an interface layer for India's monetary infrastructure. Banks that recognise this shift early and build for it will not just process the next decade’s transactions. They will shape them. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Wipro to consider first share buyback in three years at April 16 board meet, along with Q4 results- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Wipro shares settled lower in Thursday's trade at Rs 203.18 per share on the NSE, down 0.12 percent.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: IT services firm Wipro on Thursday said its Board of Directors will consider a proposal to buy back equity shares of the company at a meeting scheduled to be held on April 15–16. In a stock exchange filing, the company said the board will also deliberate on matters “necessary and incidental thereto”, in line with applicable regulations. The proposed buyback, if approved, will be undertaken in accordance with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, along with rules framed under it, and the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Buy-Back of Securities) Regulations, 2018, as amended, and other applicable laws. Further details on the size, price and mechanism of the buyback are expected to be decided by the board during the meeting. This will be the first time in nearly three years that the Bengaluru-based IT services provider evaluates a share buyback. The last such exercise was undertaken in June 2023. In that buyback, Wipro repurchased shares worth ₹12,000 crore between June 22 and June 30, acquiring 26.96 crore equity shares, or 4.91% of its total equity, at ₹445 per share. The price is not adjusted for the 1:1 bonus issue announced in December 2024. The company’s management had earlier reiterated during its third-quarter results that share buybacks would remain a key mechanism for returning excess cash to shareholders. Wipro currently holds nearly Rs 41,000 crore in net cash, amounting to close to 20% of its market capitalisation, up from Rs 28,000 crore at the time of its previous buyback, according to a note by Investec dated April 9. “Assuming a similar deployment of 20.4% of shareholders' funds, Wipro could potentially undertake a buyback of around Rs16,000 crore,” Investec said, adding that the stock offers a free cash flow yield of 7.5% to 8% for financial years 2027 and 2028. Wipro shares settled lower in Thursday's trade at Rs 203.18 per share on the NSE, down 0.12 percent. Earlier, Wipro has secured an eight-year transformation deal with Singapore-based food and agri-business major Olam Group, which is expected to exceed USD 1 billion (about Rs 9,314 crore) in contract value. As part of the broader engagement, Wipro said it will acquire Olam Group's IT and digital services business, Mindsprint Pte. Ltd., for USD 375 million (about Rs 3,494 crore). "Wipro has secured a multi-year strategic transformation deal with Olam Group, a leading USD 50+ Billion Food and Agri-business headquartered in Singapore, employing nearly 40,000 people, and majority-owned by Temasek Holdings. This 8-year deal with Olam Group is expected to exceed USD 1 billion in contract value, with a committed spend of USD 800 million. "As part of this broader engagement, Wipro will acquire Mindsprint, Olam Group's IT services arm," Wipro said in a regulatory filing on Monday. Mindsprint, founded in 2007 and headquartered in Singapore, employs over 3,200 professionals across India, Singapore, the US, the UK, and the Middle East. It reported consolidated revenues of USD 135.6 million for the calendar year 2025. The proposed sale, subject to closing adjustments, is in line with Olam Group's Updated 2025 Re-organisation Plan to responsibly divest and monetise the remaining Olam Group’s assets and businesses over time and progressively distribute the net proceeds to its shareholders via special dividends. As part of the strategic engagement, Wipro will deliver end-to-end transformation services to Olam Group through a consulting-led and AI-powered approach. The engagement will draw on Wipro's industry expertise, partnerships with leading technology providers, and Wipro Intelligence, its unified suite of AI-powered platforms, solutions and transformative offerings. The acquisition of Mindsprint, an all-cash deal for 100 per cent shareholding, is expected to be completed by June 30, 2026, subject to customary closing adjustments and regulatory approvals, including anti-trust clearances in Saudi Arabia and Australia. "Wipro's strategic engagement with Olam Group is an important step in expanding our farm-to-fork capabilities and scaling the impact of Wipro Intelligence across the food and agri-business industry. "By bringing Mindsprint's deep domain expertise and IP-led solutions, together with Wipro’s consulting-led and AI-powered capabilities, we aim to unlock growth opportunities, catalyse innovation, and drive market-ready transformation for Olam Group and our global clients across the industry," Wipro CEO and MD Srini Pallia said. Under the terms of the transaction, Mindsprint will become a wholly-owned subsidiary of Wipro. Key management personnel, led by Mindsprint CEO Suresh Sundararajan, will continue to lead the business. "As Mindsprint enters its next phase of growth with Wipro, we are confident that the combination of its deep domain expertise and Wipro's global scale and advanced capabilities will further strengthen the value it delivers to Olam Group. "This partnership positions us well to accelerate our transformation agenda and build a more agile, resilient and future-ready organisation," said Sunny Verghese, Co-Founder and CEO of Olam Group. Olam Group is a leading food and agri-business, majorly owned by Temasek Holdings. The company employs nearly 40,000 people and supplies food, ingredients, feed, and fibre to almost 22,000 customers across more than 60 countries Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: West Asia crisis: Maharashtra govt directs setting up of 24/7 helplines to expand PNG coverage- Moneycontrol.com
Description: A Government Resolution instructed companies to expand outreach and infrastructure to ensure wider PNG coverage across the state
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The Maharashtra government has directed city gas distribution companies to activate 24/7 helplines and increase the number of centres processing applications for piped natural gas connections, amid the impact of the West Asia crisis on global energy supply chains. A Government Resolution (GR), issued by the Food and Civil Supplies Department on Friday, instructed companies to expand outreach and infrastructure to ensure wider PNG coverage across the state. The GR said disruptions in global energy supply have affected petroleum product availability, leading to constraints in LPG supply, and emphasised the need to accelerate PNG connections as a short-term mitigation measure. It directed city gas distribution (CGD) companies to operationalise 24-hour helplines for consumers and ensure citizens do not face inconvenience while applying for connections. The companies were also asked to increase the number of helplines and helpdesks based on city population and rising demand. Further, the GR instructed companies to expand ward-level centres to accept new PNG connection applications and to make both online and offline application systems widely available with adequate publicity. "Housing societies, whether registered or proposed, as well as residents of constructed buildings, have been asked to submit collective or individual applications to facilitate faster rollout of PNG connections," the GR said. The government also called for establishing information and assistance centres by municipal corporations, district supply officers and CGD firms, and mandated regular district-level reviews of progress. "To monitor implementation, CGD companies must appoint district-level nodal officers and submit weekly progress reports on PNG pipeline expansion and connections, which will be consolidated and reviewed at the state level every Monday," it said. The GR said the directions will remain in force until the prevailing global geopolitical situation stabilises. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: US economy grew sluggish 0.5% in fourth quarter, says govt; downgrading previous estimate- Moneycontrol.com
Description: US gross domestic — the nation's output of goods and services — decelerated in the fourth quarter after registering impressive growth of 4.4 per cent from July through September and 3.8 per cent from April through June.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The American economy, slowed by last fall's 43-day government shutdown, grew at a sluggish 0.5 per cent annual pace from October through December, the Commerce Department reported Thursday in downgrade of its previous estimate. US gross domestic — the nation's output of goods and services — decelerated in the fourth quarter after registering impressive growth of 4.4 per cent from July through September and 3.8 per cent from April through June. The latest number was marked down from the Commerce Department's previous estimate of 0.7 per cent fourth-quarter growth. Federal government spending and investment fell at a 16.6 per cent annual pace because of the shutdown, lopping 1.16 percentage points off fourth-quarter GDP growth. Consumer spending expanded at a 1.9 per cent pace, down a notch from the previous estimate and from 3.5 per cent in the second quarter. For all of 2025, the economy grew 2.1 per cent last year, slower than 2.8 per cent in 2024 and 2.9 per cent in 2023. The economic outlook for this year is hazy after the US-Israeli war with Iran drove up energy prices and disrupted global commerce. America's job market slumped last year — recording the weakest hiring outside a recession since 2002 — but has been up and down so far in 2026: Employers added a healthy 1,60,000 jobs in January, slashed 1,33,000 in February, then created a surprising 1,78,000 in March. Thursday's report was the Commerce Department's third and final estimate of fourth-quarter GDP. The first look at January-March economic growth is due April 30. Title: Trading the ceasefire: Navigating the Iran–Israel pause: Shubham Agarwal- Moneycontrol.com
Description: If Nifty holds above key support and sentiment stabilizes, selective bullish trades make sense, especially in sectors directly hit by the conflict.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Markets love a surprise. On April 8, barely an hour before the US president's deadline expired, Washington and Tehran agreed to a two-week ceasefire. The relief was swift and violent. WTI and Brent crude posted their biggest single-day drops since April 2020. Nifty 50 surged sharply. The Rupee gained ground. For a brief moment, it felt like the world exhaled. Don't mistake that exhale for a verdict. This is not the start of a stable bull market. Traders are now entering a highly event-driven phase, where the next headline can unwind a week's gains in minutes. The big move is already in the books. The uncertainty hasn't gone anywhere. So what do you do now? Let's be clear, this ceasefire is a pause, not a resolution. Iran's parliamentary speaker stated that three clauses of the framework had already been violated, citing continued Israeli strikes in Lebanon. The Strait of Hormuz recorded only eight tanker transits on the day, well below normal traffic. Two dates now anchor every trade decision, April 11, when US-Iran talks resume in Islamabad, and April 22, when the ceasefire expires. Everything in between is a geopolitical minefield. That calls for defined-risk derivatives strategies, not naked bets. Strategy 1: Short Volatility, only if talks stabilize The ceasefire triggered an IV crush as markets repriced for de-escalation. If Islamabad talks show early progress, that crush can extend further. Trades to consider: an Iron Condor (sell OTM call and put, hedge with further OTM strikes) or an Iron Fly (sell ATM call and put, buy OTM wings as hedge). Both profit from a quiet, range-bound market. Hard rule: exit immediately on any geopolitical headline. This is a ‘calm weather only’ trade. If Nifty holds above key support and sentiment stabilizes, selective bullish trades make sense, especially in sectors directly hit by the conflict. Consider a Bull Call Spread (buy ATM call, sell OTM call) or short OTM puts paired with a hedge. The goal is measured participation, with your downside defined. Talks haven't concluded. Things can turn in any direction, in real time, with no warning. If you sell options, hedge. If you go long stocks, hedge. For every two bullish positions, carry at least one bearish offset. Volatility will stay elevated until a comprehensive agreement is in place, if one ever comes. Every strategy above uses defined risk because in event-driven markets, unhedged positions aren't trading, they're speculation. This is not a trending market. It is a reactionary market, where sentiment can reverse on a single headline before most traders have time to react. In such conditions, the best traders don't predict. They prepare. When the market is reactionary, discipline is the strategy. Size down. Hedge up. Watch the Strait of Hormuz tanker data daily. Right now, that number is your most reliable real-time ceasefire indicator. Title: Gulf uncertainty may weigh on India’s remittances, raise risk of worker return- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Kerala among most exposed as West Asia tensions threaten inflows
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Uncertainty in the Gulf region could weigh on India’s remittance inflows in FY27, which account for nearly 40 percent of total remittances, while also raising concerns about a potential return of Indian workers. Although the US and Iran announced a two-week ceasefire on April 9, there is limited clarity on its terms and implementation. Shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted, and hostilities continue in parts of the region. “Remittances are one part of the concern, particularly for states like Kerala, where a large number of workers are employed abroad. But it is not just about the flow of money. In an extreme situation, you could also see workers returning, and that creates a different kind of pressure,” a senior government official told Moneycontrol. “What we are hearing from the ground is that there could be a significant impact on remittances. Flows could decline sharply, as workers may hold back transfers or return depending on how the situation evolves,” the official added. A Moneycontrol analysis shows that Kerala remains among the most vulnerable states due to its heavy reliance on remittances. While the state received $23.4 billion in inward remittances in 2023-24, the second-highest after Maharashtra, transfers accounted for 17.1 percent of Kerala’s GDP, the highest among major states and more than three times Maharashtra’s roughly 5 percent share. The national average stood at about 3 percent. This dependence means any disruption in the Gulf, where a large share of Kerala’s diaspora is employed, could directly affect household incomes, consumption and local economic activity. By comparison, other large states are less dependent relative to their economic size. Tamil Nadu received about $12.3 billion in remittances (3.8 percent of GDP) in FY24, while Telangana and Karnataka received $9.6 billion and $9.1 billion, respectively. Shifting geography, persistent dependence At the national level, the geography of remittances has been gradually shifting. According to the RBI’s Remittance Survey 2023-24, the US had overtaken the UAE as India’s largest source of remittances, with its share rising from 22.9 percent in 2016-17 to 27.7 percent in 2023-24. The UAE’s share declined from 26.9 percent to 19.2 percent, while the UK’s share rose sharply from 3 percent to 10.9 percent, overtaking Saudi Arabia. India received about $129 billion in remittances in 2024, accounting for roughly 14.3 percent of global flows, more than the next two recipient countries combined. In FY25, inflows rose further to $135.5 billion. The Gulf remains a critical corridor, accounting for about 38 percent of India’s remittance inflows, with nearly half of that coming from the UAE. The World Bank, in its report released on April 8, also flagged this concern that South Asia remains more exposed to Middle East remittance flows than most other emerging market regions. The region is also home to around 9 million overseas Indians, including large populations in the UAE (3.4 million), Saudi Arabia (2.59 million), Kuwait (1.03 million), Oman (0.78 million) and Qatar (0.75 million). For now, policymakers remain cautiously optimistic. “We are not anticipating a dent to remittances if the crisis is resolved soon,” RBI Governor Sanjay Malhotra said on April 8. World Bank India economist Aurélien Kruse also noted that remittances may not be impacted much by the current crisis. An extension of conflict or delay in return to normalcy could change that outlook. The World Bank predicts the Middle East region, excluding Iran, to grow 1.8 percent this year compared with 4.2 percent projected in January. Title: Govt prepares to float Rabi fertiliser tenders, flags weak supply visibility, rising logistics costs- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Hoarding of fertilisers concerns emerge as officials flag over-buying by farmers and dealers amid uncertainty. States asked to step in to regulate distribution and prevent supply distortions.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: India is preparing to float tenders for Rabi fertiliser procurement, but supply visibility remains weak due to uncertainty over supplier participation, logistics costs, insurance, and shipping routes, a senior government source said, even as Kharif stocks remain comfortable and budgetary support is in place. “For the Rabi season, fertiliser tenders have to be issued now. The requirement is immediate and funds are available, but the key question is who will supply. Where will the suppliers come from, and which players are willing to participate -- these are the uncertainties we are dealing with,” the senior government official said. Rabi fertilisers are primarily required between October and November, for which global import tenders need to be issued now. The tenders are mainly for phosphatic and potassic fertilisers, though limited urea imports may also be undertaken to bridge domestic gaps, where India remains dependent on global suppliers. Officials said issuing tenders does not ensure timely availability, especially amid disruptions in global trade flows. “You can be ready and say that you want to float a fertiliser tender, but that does not guarantee supply. You may not get the material in time for the Rabi cycle. Timing is critical, and even if the tender is issued, deliveries may not align with seasonal requirements,” the source said. “Even if tenders are floated, the question is who will supply and at what cost. Logistics expenses, insurance availability, and shipping routes -- whether cargo has to move via the Cape of Good Hope or other alternatives -- are all adding to the uncertainty in commercial agreements,” the official added. “There is a lot of uncertainty in these contracts right now. Several variables are not fully known or cannot be locked in at the time of tendering, which makes procurement more complicated than usual,” the source said. The government indicated that prior planning has helped cushion immediate stress. “We had made some preparations in advance -- ensuring adequate fertiliser stocking and maintaining strong fundamentals -- and that is helping us manage the situation better despite these uncertainties,” the official said. The Centre had earlier approved a subsidy outlay of Rs 41,534 crore for phosphatic and potassic fertilisers for the Kharif 2026 season to ensure availability and price stability amid volatile global input costs. Officials have also indicated that the overall fertiliser subsidy bill could rise to around Rs 2.20 lakh crore in FY27 due to higher energy prices and geopolitical tensions. Alongside global procurement challenges, domestic distribution distortions are beginning to surface. “There is some truth to the concern that people are hoarding fertilisers. They are buying much more than what they actually require. There is clearly an element of over-purchasing,” the source said. To address this, the Centre has asked state governments to ensure equitable distribution. “State governments have now been told to step in and ensure that fertilisers are released into the market. They have been advised to manage distribution carefully so that supplies reach where they are actually needed,” the source said. “You cannot allow indiscriminate stocking when every bag of fertiliser is being imported and supplied at cost. Distribution has to be tightly managed, otherwise it will distort availability and put additional pressure on the system,” the official added. India relies significantly on imports for key fertilisers such as di-ammonium phosphate (DAP), muriate of potash (MOP), and a portion of its urea requirements, making availability sensitive to global price movements and shipping disruptions. In this backdrop, policymakers face a dual challenge -- securing timely imports while ensuring balanced domestic distribution. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Chartist Talk: Nifty back above 24,000, but the bigger trade may now be in midcaps and smallcaps- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Looking ahead, technical signals point toward the possibility of further extension in the Nifty 50 pullback rally, with immediate upside targets placed at 24,300 and 24,500. On the downside, the 23,650–23,600 zone is expected to act as a key support level, said Sudeep Shah of SBI Securities.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Technical observations suggest that the broader indices are well placed to extend their outperformance in the near term, said Sudeep Shah, the Head of Technical and Derivatives Research at SBI Securities in an interview to Moneycontrol. According to him, the Nifty Midcap 100 is displaying a very strong setup, as it is currently trading above all its key short term as well as long term moving averages, while the Nifty Smallcap 100 index' daily RSI is already placed above the 60 level and continues to trend higher, further confirming bullish momentum. After stellar rally of 5-7 percent on Friday, Sudeep Shah believes uptrend in Adani Energy Solutions is likely to remain intact, while Siemens continues to trade above its key short and long-term moving averages, reinforcing the underlying strength, and Lenskart Solutions is well-positioned to extend its upward move in the coming sessions if it sees follow-through buying from current levels. What is the likelihood of the Nifty 50 holding the 24,000 level next week, based on a detailed chart analysis? What are the levels to watch? The benchmark index Nifty staged a strong pullback rally over the past week, closing above the 24,000 mark with a gain of 5.89 percent, marking its best weekly performance since February 2021, following a two-week ceasefire between the US and Iran. From its recent swing low of 22,182, the index rebounded sharply by 8.19 percent within just six trading sessions. However, the more important aspect of this move lies in the underlying drivers of this strength. The rally has been supported by a clear improvement in market participation. Market breadth expanded significantly, reflecting widespread buying interest across sectors. Notably, the broader markets outpaced the headline indices, with both the Nifty Midcap 100 and Nifty Smallcap 100 rising over 7 percent each and forming strong bullish candles—suggesting that the real momentum is building beneath the surface. Technically, Nifty has now moved above its 20-day EMA, which has started to slope upward. At the same time, the previously declining 50, 100, and 200-day EMAs are beginning to flatten, indicating a possible shift in the overall trend. Momentum indicators are also turning favourable—the daily RSI has climbed back to around 54 and is trading above its 9-day average, while the MACD histogram shows a pickup in positive momentum. That said, the key question remains whether this momentum can sustain going forward. Looking ahead, these technical signals point toward the possibility of further extension in the pullback rally, with immediate upside targets placed at 24,300 and 24,500. On the downside, the 23,650–23,600 zone is expected to act as a key support level. Have you observed any significant changes in FII positioning following the Iran ceasefire and the cooling of oil prices? Encouragingly, after persistent selling over 27 consecutive sessions amounting to a massive Rs 1,72,656 crore outflow, FIIs finally turned net buyers on Friday, with inflows of Rs 672 crore. In the preceding three sessions, the intensity of selling had already begun to moderate, culminating in this shift towards buying. While the quantum of buying remains relatively small compared to the earlier outflows, the change in stance is a positive signal, especially as the Nifty has been showing early signs of a reversal over the past six trading sessions. On the derivatives front, the long-short ratio improved to 22.06% from 16.84% last Friday, indicating gradual short covering by FIIs in index futures. Although both the buying figure and the extent of short covering are modest, the undertone is turning constructive. Sustained buying activity along with meaningful short covering from current levels could provide the necessary momentum to push the Nifty higher in the coming week. Do you expect the Bank Nifty to climb decisively above the 57,000 mark next week? Bank Nifty has emerged as a clear outperformer versus the broader indices over the past week, underscoring strong leadership from the banking space. The index posted a sharp weekly gain of 8.47 percent, registering its best weekly performance in the last couple of years. Technically, the weekly chart shows the formation of a strong bullish candle, highlighting decisive buying interest and a solid rebound from recent lows. The index is now trading comfortably above its 20-day EMA, indicating a favourable shift in the near-term trend. Momentum indicators also align with this strength. The daily RSI is placed at 53.91 and is gradually inching higher, suggesting building momentum and improved participation across banking stocks. In the near term, Bank Nifty is expected to continue its pullback rally, with potential upside targets at 56,700 and 57,500 if momentum sustains. On the downside, the 54,700–54,600 zone is likely to act as a key support band, and holding above this level will be crucial to maintain a positive bias. Do you see stronger momentum in the broader markets compared to the benchmark indices? Over the past week, both the broader market indices—Nifty Midcap 100 and Nifty Smallcap 100—have significantly outperformed the frontline indices, posting sharp weekly gains of over 7 percent each. Notably, this marks their highest weekly performance recorded over the last couple of years. The outperformance of the broader indices is not just limited to the last week; they have been consistently outperforming the benchmark Nifty over the past few weeks. This trend is well reflected in their relative strength (ratio) charts against the Nifty, which continue to form a clear sequence of higher tops and higher bottoms, indicating sustained relative strength and improving risk appetite. From a technical standpoint, the Nifty Midcap 100 is displaying a very strong setup, as it is currently trading above all its key short term as well as long term moving averages. The daily RSI is close to crossing above the 60 mark and remains in a rising trajectory, highlighting strengthening momentum. Meanwhile, the Nifty Smallcap 100 is trading above its 20-day and 50-day EMA. Its daily RSI is already placed above the 60 level and continues to trend higher, further confirming bullish momentum. Collectively, these technical observations suggest that the broader indices are well placed to extend their outperformance in the near term. Which two stocks would you consider buying for next week? Nippon Life had been consolidating within the Rs 796–892 range on the daily chart before giving a decisive breakout above the upper band. This move was followed by strong follow-through buying in subsequent sessions, reinforcing bullish sentiment. Momentum indicators support the uptrend, with RSI trending higher and the DI+ crossing above DI- on the ADX, indicating strengthening buying pressure and momentum. Additionally, the rising ratio line in Capital Market/Nifty ratio chart highlights sectoral outperformance, positioning the stock as a potential leader in the ongoing rally. Hence, we recommend accumulating the stock in the zone of Rs 960-950 with a stop-loss of Rs 910. On the upside, it is likely to test the level of Rs 1,030 in the short term. Cummins India has given a stage-2 cup pattern breakout on the daily chart, signaling a potential shift in trend. The move was followed by a sharp follow through move, supported by a notable surge in volumes, indicating strong participation. Momentum indicators further validate the bullish bias, with DI+ crossing above DI- on the ADX and MACD moving above the signal line, while RSI continues to trend higher. The overall setup suggests the stock is well positioned to extend its up move in the near term. Hence, we recommend accumulating the stock in the zone of Rs 5,140-5,100 with a stop-loss of Rs 4,980. On the upside, it is likely to test the level of Rs 5,500 in the short term. Do you expect the rally to continue in Adani Energy, Siemens, and Lenskart Solutions next week? Adani Energy has delivered a decisive breakout above a downward-sloping trendline on the daily chart, backed by strong follow-through buying and healthy volumes. Momentum indicators remain supportive. RSI is trending higher and sustaining above 60, reflecting strong bullish momentum. Additionally, the DI+ line has crossed above DI- on the ADX indicator, signaling clear buyer dominance. As long as the stock holds above the Rs 1,090–1,085 support zone, the uptrend is likely to remain intact, with potential for further upside in the near term. Siemens has witnessed a sharp pullback of nearly 16.5% over the last six trading sessions, indicating renewed buying interest. The stock continues to trade above its key short and long-term moving averages, reinforcing the underlying strength. RSI is also trending higher, suggesting improving momentum. However, the stock is currently hovering near a crucial resistance zone. A sustained move above this level, supported by strong volumes, could trigger the next leg of the rally. Lenskart has broken out of a prolonged consolidation range of Rs 544–481 on the daily chart, indicating a potential trend reversal. This breakout is further validated by a downward-sloping trendline breakout on the RSI, signaling strengthening bullish momentum. If the stock sees follow-through buying from current levels, it is well-positioned to extend its upward move in the coming sessions. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Boeing’s moon rocket faces uncertain future under Trump’s NASA- Moneycontrol.com
Description: About a week before the $24 billion Space Launch System pushed the four crew members of the Artemis II mission around the moon, NASA asked rivals what options they could offer for its ambitious plan of future lunar trips.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: NASA’s Boeing Co. rocket just propelled astronauts farther into space than ever before. The Trump administration is already looking to competitors for a replacement. About a week before the $24 billion Space Launch System pushed the four crew members of the Artemis II mission around the moon, NASA asked rivals what options they could offer for its ambitious plan of future lunar trips. That call, echoed almost immediately in the White House’s budget request, put a big question mark on the future of Boeing’s beleaguered rocket after roughly a decade of development. The fate of the program — worth tens of billions of dollars over the next few years — has become a key test for Jared Isaacman, the billionaire fintech entrepreneur who President Donald Trump named to run NASA last year, in his efforts to make the space agency faster and more efficient. He’s counting on new commercial companies like SpaceX to provide cheaper alternatives to the costly systems like SLS developed by legacy players like Boeing and Lockheed Martin Corp. “Because that program draws on such history, has contractors, hundreds of subcontractors, tens of thousands of people, it’s expensive,” Isaacman said in February. “It’s not the vehicle that you are going to take to and from the moon a couple of times a year as you build out a moon base the way the president wants.” That network of support — Artemis counts suppliers in all 50 states — has helped the program survive efforts to kill it over years of delays and cost overruns. The administration’s attempt to phase out the SLS and the Lockheed-made Orion crew capsule in its budget request last year ran into fierce opposition on Capitol Hill, where lawmakers ultimately succeeded in blocking the cuts. Last week, the White House signaled that it will try again to find commercial replacements. With a 2028 deadline looming to land astronauts on the moon before Trump leaves office and China planning its own mission by the end of the decade, Isaacman is under pressure to deliver. Although legacy providers like Boeing have struggled to meet deadlines in the past, their technologies are proven. New rivals like SpaceX and Blue Origin have yet to show their rockets can get to the moon. In February, he announced that NASA would be canceling Boeing’s multi-billion dollar contract for a more powerful upper stage for the SLS rocket despite years of development. In March, he announced a pause on Gateway, a planned space station in lunar orbit, leaving international partners and companies involved scrambling to adjust. In its place, he outlined plans for a base on the moon’s surface and an accelerated slate of missions to build it. “He is really trying to rely heavily on commercial space and competition,” said Dave Cavossa, president of the Commercial Space Federation, which represents companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin. “I think it’s the most pro-commercial administration, the most pro-change administration leadership we’ve ever seen.” Artemis was created in the first Trump administration out of the remnants of a NASA program that had been cancelled by his predecessor but managed to limp along thanks to continued funding from Congress. By the time Trump returned to the White House last year, the holdups and price tag had grown. A focus of the criticism is the SLS rocket, which has carried the Artemis missions into orbit at a cost of about $4 billion per trip — four times initial estimates and years behind schedule. “We are not going to sit idly by when schedules slip or budgets are exceeded,” Isaacman said on Mar. 24. “Expect uncomfortable action if that is what it takes, because the public has invested over 100 billion dollars and has been very patient with respect to America’s return to the moon.” A Boeing spokesperson said that the company is a proud partner in the Artemis mission. Tony Byers, Director of Orion Exploration Services and Transformation at Lockheed Martin, said that the Orion spacecraft is the only flight-proven deep space crew vehicle and that the company would continue to evolve the capsule to meet NASA’s planned increased flight cadence. NASA didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment. When the White House proposed winding down SLS and the Orion spacecraft after just three flights in its budget request to Congress last May, lobbyists from contractors like Boeing and Lockheed Martin flooded Capitol Hill. They targeted Texas Senator Ted Cruz and Representative Brian Babin, whose districts rely heavily on the programs for jobs. By July, Cruz led a push to reinstate about $6.7 billion to keep the program funded even as Republicans were lining up behind most of Trump’s other priorities. “It speaks to the strength of the program to some key members of Congress and then those key members really acting to show that strength,” said Mike French, founder of the consulting firm Space Policy Group. This year, the administration’s budget proposal doesn’t include a hard deadline for phasing out SLS and Orion, just the vaguer request to look for commercial alternatives. NASA also said it’s assessing other options for Artemis missions set to launch after 2028. For the moment, SLS is the only rocket on the market that can do what NASA needs. The lack of other options has allowed lawmakers to walk a tightrope between embracing a commercial alternative and defending the legacy architecture for now. “I think we need to use what we have,” Babin said, pointing at the SLS rocket standing behind him at Kennedy Space Center on Apr. 1 shortly before the launch of Artemis II. “When we have an alternative, I think that would be great to have a commercial rocket or a government-owned rocket, whatever it takes.” Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Techie quits after 14 years to take up farming. 10 years on, he says it's fulfilling but financially tough- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Despite the challenges, the former techie said he does not regret the move. Moments like walking through fields during the monsoon, eating lunch after physical labour and watching crops grow offer fulfilment that desk jobs never did.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Quitting a stable tech job to take up farming is often romanticised as a move towards freedom, peace and a closer connection with nature. For one former software engineer, however, the reality has proved far more complex — demanding, uncertain and financially fragile, yet rewarding in its own way. In a detailed Reddit post, a user who goes by lastodyssey said he spent 14 years working as a software engineer before switching to full-time farming a decade ago. After nearly 10 years in agriculture, he says the decision has brought satisfaction, but at a cost many who dream of a similar path tend to underestimate. “Farming is hard — both physically and mentally,” he wrote, cautioning corporate professionals who fantasise about green fields, freedom, flexible schedules and growing their own food. According to him, farming offers no shortcuts to profitability. Margins are thin, and the moment labour costs enter the picture, profits often evaporate. “If you do it on your own, you may get a profit. Hire labour, you may just break even — or make a loss,” he explained. Scale plays a crucial role. Smaller farms carry lower risk but limited upside, while larger landholdings increase both returns and exposure to loss. Crop selection adds another layer of risk, with short-term crops like millets and maize, medium-term options like banana, and long-term fruit plantations each carrying distinct financial and climatic uncertainties. Even when farmers do everything right, external factors can undo months of effort. He pointed to how banana growers have suffered losses due to export disruptions triggered by geopolitical developments such as the Middle East conflict. “Unknown variables are too many,” he noted. Read more: Infosys techie, 27, quits job to take up brinjal farming in Japan, earns double Unlike salaried jobs, farming operates on delayed outcomes. Short-term crop cycles last four to six months, during which capital is continuously invested while income arrives only at the end — if at all. “Input now, output later,” he summed up, adding that farmers often wait months to learn whether their choices around seeds, fertilisers or timing were correct. Adding to the pressure is the dependence on intermediaries. “Middleman is the king,” he wrote, describing how pricing power rarely rests with farmers. One of his strongest warnings was against impulsive land purchases. Farming land, he argued, appreciates slowly unless development intervenes and cannot be sold quickly during distress. His advice to beginners was blunt: lease land first. “Do not buy to test waters,” he wrote, urging aspiring farmers to preserve capital, keep farm finances separate and maintain a secondary income. Beyond economics, the shift demanded major lifestyle changes. Living in a tier-2 or tier-3 city, reduced purchasing power and fewer amenities became part of daily life. “Purchasing power is gone,” he admitted, adding that he now avoids unnecessary spending and focuses on repairing rather than replacing items. Despite the challenges, he said he does not regret the move. Moments like walking through fields during the monsoon, eating lunch after physical labour and watching crops grow offer fulfilment that desk jobs never did. “I dread traffic in tier-1 cities,” he wrote. “I would rather do farming and live in a tier-2 city.” He rated his life satisfaction at seven out of ten, saying financial stability remains the missing piece. While stories of engineers making crores in farming grab headlines, he warned such cases are rare and often misleading. “Do it if you have money, time and clear goals,” he concluded — stripping away fantasy to leave a grounded reality. Read more: This 24-year-old quit his Rs 1.5 crore pa job for farming: ‘Worked 15 hours a day, now life is bindaas' The Reddit post drew mixed reactions. Many users said it was one of the rare accounts that clearly separated the romance of farming from its economics. Others admitted they dreamed of a similar life but were cautious after seeing family members struggle as farmers. “My father-in-law is a farmer and always tells me not to get into it,” one user commented, echoing concerns around low profits and middlemen. Another summed it up succinctly: “Most people underestimate how much of farming is actually risk management.” Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Price Action: New India Assurance, KPR Mills rally over 5% even as IT stocks decline- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Among the gainers, banking stocks traded higher, with ICICI Bank, Axis Bank and Punjab National Bank rising around 2 percent each. Federal Bank, Canara Bank, HDFC Bank, Kotak Mahindra Bank, State Bank of India and IndusInd Bank gained about 1.5 percent each.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Indian equities gained sharply on April 10, with investors turning cautiously optimistic ahead of the upcoming US-Iran talks this weekend. Among the gainers, banking stocks traded higher, with ICICI Bank, Axis Bank and Punjab National Bank rising around 2 percent each. Federal Bank, Canara Bank, HDFC Bank, Kotak Mahindra Bank, State Bank of India, and IndusInd Bank gained about 1.5 percent each. Midcap and smallcap stocks continued to see selective interest. New India Assurance surged 13 percent, while KPR Mills and Stral advanced over 4 percent each. Berger Paints, AIA Engineering, and Cochin Shipyard gained more than 3 percent. On the downside, IT stocks came under pressure following Tata Consultancy Services’ earnings. Mphasis fell 3.5 percent, Coforge declined 2.5 percent, Persistent Systems dropped 1.6 percent, while Oracle Financial Services Software slipped 1 percent. Sectorally, realty stocks outperformed, with the index rising 2 percent, while the IT index lagged, declining 2.3 percent. Market breadth remained strong, with 3,052 stocks advancing and 491 declining on the BSE, indicating broad-based participation. Traders said the market continues to remain stock-specific, with earnings visibility and news flow driving price action. While large caps remain relatively stable, midcaps and small caps are witnessing sharper moves on both sides. Volatility remains contained, but participants remain cautious amid mixed global cues and ahead of key domestic triggers. Going ahead, stock-specific developments, earnings announcements, and global trends are expected to guide market direction in the near term. Title: Petrol, diesel prices today, April 12: Fuel rates steady amid global crude tensions, check city-wise prices- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Petrol and diesel prices remain unchanged as global crude markets stay volatile amid ongoing West Asia tensions.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Petrol and diesel prices on April 12, 2026: Fuel rates across major Indian cities remained unchanged on Sunday, even as global crude oil markets remain on edge amid ongoing tensions in West Asia and disruptions along key shipping routes. Despite continued volatility in international oil prices, there has been no immediate impact on domestic retail fuel rates, which have stayed stable across key cities. The three major oil marketing companies (OMCs) in India — Indian Oil Corporation (IOC), Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited (BPCL), and Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited (HPCL) — revise fuel prices daily to reflect changes in global crude oil prices and currency exchange rate movements. However, prices have remained steady in recent sessions. In Delhi, petrol continues to be priced at Rs 94.77 per litre, while diesel stands at Rs 87.67 per litre. Prices remain higher in Mumbai, where petrol is retailing at Rs 103.49 per litre and diesel at around Rs 90.03 per litre. Among major metro cities, petrol prices remain above Rs 100 per litre in most locations, including Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad and Kolkata, while diesel prices continue to remain below the Rs 100 mark. Fuel prices in India are revised daily based on movements in global crude oil prices and changes in foreign exchange rates. Despite volatility in global crude markets, petrol and diesel prices in India have remained largely stable over the past few years. Retail fuel prices have seen limited movement since May 2022, when excise duties and state taxes were reduced. What affects petrol and diesel prices in India? Retail fuel prices are influenced by multiple factors, including global crude oil prices, the rupee–dollar exchange rate, central and state taxes, transportation costs, and demand–supply dynamics. Since India imports a large share of its crude oil, any fluctuation in global prices or currency movements can impact domestic fuel rates.
