Title: Govt begins IDBI Bank's revaluation process in renewed push for sale- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Decision on bids likely in a month after valuation exercise. Subdued stock price, weak bids prompt rethink of strategy
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The government has initiated a fresh valuation of IDBI Bank and will take a call on inviting bids only after the exercise is completed in about a month, a senior government official said, signalling a reset of the disinvestment process. The revised valuation will serve as the benchmark for any future stake sale. “These are difficult times for the transaction. We are still in the process of valuation and no decision has been taken yet. The fresh valuation itself will take about a month to complete, and only after that will we be in a position to take a call,” the official told Moneycontrol. The development follows the earlier round of the strategic sale not yielding expected outcomes, prompting the Centre to reassess pricing expectations amid a weaker market environment. The government and Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC), which together hold a majority stake in IDBI Bank, are looking to recalibrate the transaction to ensure better value realisation. “At this stage, it has not been decided whether fresh expressions of interest will be invited again. That is something we will assess only after the valuation exercise is completed and we have greater clarity on the way forward,” the official said. There has been a sharp correction in IDBI Bank’s share price – from around Rs 118 at the peak of disinvestment optimism to the low Rs 70s after the deal lost momentum – while bids from Fairfax India Holdings and Emirates NBD reportedly fell short of internal valuation benchmarks. Sources indicated that the current market price is a key constraint in moving ahead with the transaction. “The current equity price is also on the lower side, and that has to be factored in. You cannot proceed with a transaction of this nature without aligning expectations on valuation, especially when market conditions are not very supportive,” the official said. The decline in the stock has effectively narrowed the government’s room to negotiate, raising concerns that proceeding with a sale at current levels could lead to value erosion for public shareholders. Shift in strategy: from hype to calibrated approach The government is recalibrating its approach to the next phase of the sale, moving away from the earlier strategy that saw heightened market signalling and a sharp rally in the stock. “The global environment is not very supportive right now. Earlier, there was a lot of hype around the transaction and things were progressing well, but the situation has changed since then,” the official said. The next attempt at the IDBI Bank disinvestment is expected to be more calibrated, with an emphasis on avoiding speculative price movements and ensuring valuations remain aligned with fundamentals. “Going ahead, the approach will have to be more measured. We will need to work quietly rather than build expectations too early. That, at this point, appears to be the most appropriate way forward,” the official added. The current exercise reflects a broader reset in the government’s disinvestment strategy for the lender. The Centre had in 2022 invited EoIs for selling a 60.72 percent stake in IDBI Bank, including management control, marking one of the most significant privatisation efforts in the banking sector. However, as seen in other large-ticket disinvestment attempts, including BPCL and Shipping Corporation of India, market conditions have played a critical role in shaping outcomes. In the case of IDBI Bank, weak investor appetite appears to have necessitated a reassessment. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Early-stage VC investments in Q1FY23 up 30% on year even as growth-stage investments come under pressure- Moneycontrol.com
Description: However, late-stage investments fell over 11% to $9.1 billion during the first quarter of 2022-23 from $10.3 billion a year earlier, according to data compiled by analytics firm Tracxn Technologies.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Early-stage venture capital (VC) investments in India rose by nearly a third in the first quarter of 2022-23 (FY23) from a year earlier as investors turned cautious and made smaller-sized and longer-duration bets amid a correction in global financial markets. In April-June 2022, early-stage VC investments in India (up to Series A rounds) rose over 28 percent to $1.50 billion from $1.17 billion a year earlier, according to data compiled by data analytics firm Tracxn Technologies. The data also showed that the average size of early-stage deals more than doubled to $3.94 million in the first quarter of FY23 from $1.92 million in April-June 2021. With central banks across the globe raising interest rates to tame soaring inflation, global financial markets have slumped over the last three months. Consequently, large investors are cutting their exposure to high-growth technology companies, and thus tech valuations across the world have been hit the hardest. VC firms, including some of the world’s most aggressive investors, including Tiger Global Management and SoftBank, have said that they will cut their investments this year. VCs are thus investing more actively at early stages, which are typically smaller deals and have longer-duration cycles. Moneycontrol had reported earlier this week that Tiger Global Management, one of the world’s most aggressive tech investors, had upped its Series A investments in India this year , in what was a shift in strategy for the New York-based Hedge fund company, which typically invests at later stages. The shift in strategy came at a time when the value of the company’s public stock holdings slumped to $26 billion at the end of the March quarter. “Over the last six-eight months, Tiger Global, Falcon Edge and all such funds, which have a hedge-fund approach, have gotten burnt very badly with the market coming down. So, they are going to be cash-strapped and are going to hold on to their investments. They might not stop, but I am given to understand that they might slow down their investments,” said Vikram Gupta, founder and managing partner, IvyCap Ventures Advisors. “And if you look at the bigger startup picture, out of the over 60,000 registered startups, about 25,000 have got angel, seed or pre-Series A funding. But only about 1,200 are Series A funded, and of those, 100+ have translated into being unicorns,” Gupta said. “So, there’s that huge potential for building many more unicorns. But there are challenges at Series A. You need conviction, cost is high, fund sizes are small, fund economics don’t allow you to hire good quality resources, among others. So, there’s a lot of gap at Series A and funds are looking to disrupt that, and so are we,” he added. Gupta’s comments come at a time when some of the world’s biggest VC firms, such as Sequoia Capital, Matrix Partners, Accel and Elevation Capital, which invest at early stages, have raised or are in the process of raising their largest-ever India-focused funds, suggesting more dry powder for early-stage investments. Sequoia Capital India, which recently raised $2.8 billion in a fund for Indian and Southeast Asian startups, has earmarked $300 million for early-stage investments through Surge, its startup accelerator programme. The VC firm also recently raised the ceiling for its seed-level investments through Surge to as much as $3 million. “Angel investing in India is exploding — there’s tremendous interest for Series A investments in India from institutional investors. India needs more capital. So, while we are caught in this notion of competition, we are still one-tenth in terms of venture investing in the world, so there’s obviously a lot of room for more capital to come in,” said Vani Kola, founder of Kalaari capital. “We should actively collaborate with this capital that is coming in, there are micro VC funds, which have a size of about $10 million, and they also have a role to play here because founders need capital at different stages of their companies’ life,” Kola added. Late-stage investments (Series B onwards), meanwhile, fell over 11 percent to $9.1 billion during the first quarter of 2022-23 (FY23) from $10.3 billion a year earlier, the data showed. The average size of late-stage deals, meanwhile, was little changed at $75 million in April-June 2022 from $77 million in the first quarter of FY21. Late-stage deals had fallen below the $1 billion-mark in May 2022 for the first time in about 12 months, but in June 2022, late-stage deals regained momentum, and topped $1.8 billion, the data showed. “I would say early-stage investments have not slowed down at all. I don’t know if they have picked up, but they haven’t slowed down at all and I don’t think they will slow down either,” Kola said. “Late-stage investments and growth-stage investments, in fact, have slowed down considerably. When you talk about early-stage investments, India has tremendous potential over the next 10 years, so we are very bullish and so are other investors, so I don’t think early-stage investments will slow down. We are looking to do what we did last year, investing about $75-90 million dollars across these deals,” Kola added. Padmaja Ruparel, co-founder of Indian Angel Network, meanwhile, said that she expects overall funding to slow down compared to last year but added that companies across stages that are able to project stronger financial metrics will be able to raise capital irrespective of the macroeconomic situation. “I would say what happened last year was unprecedented and that obviously won’t be the case this year,” said Ruparel. “But having said that, investors’ job is to invest so they will continue doing that. The competition for term sheets will be less intense, and valuations will drop, which is the case already for some companies. But overall, it (funding) will not end suddenly. Companies with stronger unit economics and those in early stages that are able to show a clear path to profitability will be able to raise funds,” Ruparel added. Title: Chartist Talk: Is Nifty’s technical rebound still alive? Milan Vaishnav says 24,000 is possible, bets on these two stocks- Moneycontrol.com
Description: If the Nifty 50 consolidates, Milan Vaishnav expects the 23,000 level to stay defended.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Milan Vaishnav, the CMT and founder of Gemstone Equity Research & Advisory Services feels that the current counter-trend rally in the Nifty 50 still has some steam left in it. If there are no incremental negatives to deal with, this is a major caveat, then this relief rally can itself move to 24,000 level, he said. Even if the market consolidates, he expects the 23,000 level to stay defended. For the Bank Nifty, he believes the 55,500, the recent swing high, may act as a a short-term resistance for the index. "This index has shown bullish divergence on the lead indicator, and in the event of the Nifty extending its rally, we may even see Nifty Bank relatively outperforming the Nifty," he said. Do you expect the strong signals from charts and pattern formations to indicate a further rally in the Nifty 50? First of all, I will not call this a rally; in the strictest sense, what we are having right now is a technical rebound. This is something we can say is a "counter-trend" rally, in a way. That said, we need to understand that we are currently below all key moving averages on the daily chart. Zooming out a bit, it is also evident that we have decisively violated the 100-week MA, which is presently at 24,461. Now, coming to the sustainability of the rally or a technical rebound. Yes, I feel that the current counter-trend rally still has some steam left in it. If there are no incremental negatives to deal with, this is a major caveat, then this relief rally can itself move to 24,000 level. What appears from the derivatives data is that if we add up the next two expiries, including the one that falls on Monday, the strike of 23,000 has accumulated maximum Put open inerest. Also, this place has seen significant Call unwinding as well. So even if we consolidate, I expect the 23,000 level to stay defended. What are the key levels to watch for the Nifty’s next move, and what is your trading strategy from here? The trading strategy would be simple. I will intend to ride the technical rebound. I will expect 23,800 to 24,000 on Nifty. In the process, I will keep 22,900 as my protective stop. Do you think the Bank Nifty can rally above the 56,000 level in the upcoming sessions? I would put that figure to 55,500 and not 56,000. This is the most recent swing high, and that may provide a short-term resistance to the Nifty Bank Index. This index has shown bullish divergence on the lead indicator, and in the event of the Nifty extending its rally, we may even see Nifty Bank relatively outperforming the Nifty. What are your top two picks for the short term? Asian Paints has shown strong bullish divergence of the RSI against the price during its last phase of decline. In the process, it has effectively put a bottom in place for itself. There is a potential upside appreciation of around 7 percent from the previous close price of Rs 2,270. Adani Power , too, has shown divergences on the OBV (on-balance volume). This volume oscillator has formed a new high ahead of the price breakout, indicating strong accumulation in the stock. From the previous close of Rs 153.65, this stock can potentially return 7-8 percent over the coming weeks. In both cases, we will need to maintain a protective stop at around 4 percent below the buy price to maintain a healthy risk-reward ratio. Are you betting on HDFC Bank and Shriram Finance based on chart signals? HDFC Bank is a good portfolio addition. It may not be the best stock to trade, but it can certainly be entered into for a decent alpha generation from an 18 to 24-month investment horizon. Shriram Finance is something that can consolidate in a wide range. Its technical structure differs from that of HDFC Bank. In this case, so long as Shriram Finance stays above Rs 890, one can stay invested in it; below which one will need to exit. Is this the right time to buy Kalyan Jewellers and PI Industries ? At best, Kalyan Jewellers may see a technical rebound continuing a bit more. Otherwise, it still remains in a broader multi-month downtrend. One may hold on to this stock with a stop-loss placed below Rs 360; however, fresh exposure is something I would not recommend. The same goes with PI Industries. There are no indications of any major trend reversals; some technical rebound may happen, but the stock is far from showing any major change of trend. One may hold with a protective stop-loss placed below Rs 2,770, but a fresh entry is not recommended here. FIIs have been net sellers throughout this month. However, they continue to sit on a net short position, and the slightest of the external triggers may have them participate in a sharp short-covering move. Title: Adani Group plans strategic split of Jaypee assets after insolvency takeover- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The deal hands the Adani Group control over a diverse portfolio spanning cement plants, power assets, large land parcels in the Delhi-NCR region, and the Buddh International Circuit, the iconic Formula One racing track
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The Adani Group is likely to undertake a significant restructuring of Jaiprakash Associates Ltd (JAL) following its successful bid to acquire the bankrupt company, carving up assets across its core verticals to unlock value and streamline operations, according to people familiar with the development. The resolution plan, approved on March 17 by the Allahabad bench of the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), involves an acquisition outlay of about Rs 14,535 crore against JAL’s admitted debt of roughly Rs 57,185 crore. Adani Enterprises Ltd was the resolution applicant on behalf of the Adani Group, whose plan was approved by the tribunal. The deal hands the Adani Group control over a diverse portfolio spanning cement plants, power assets, large land parcels in the Delhi-NCR region, and the Buddh International Circuit, the iconic Formula One racing track. As part of its post-acquisition strategy, the group is expected to redistribute these assets internally across its business verticals rather than house them under a single umbrella under its post-acquisition strategy, sources said. Jaypee’s power assets are expected to be transferred to Adani Power at a pre-agreed enterprise value, in keeping with the group’s strategy of consolidating generation assets under a single platform, sources said. The hospitality portfolio, which includes hotel properties as well as the substantial land bank in the Delhi-NCR region, is expected to be retained under the group’s real estate arm, Adani Realty, which is directly owned by the promoter family. The transaction is expected to make Adani Realty one of the largest land bank owners in the NCR region, while significantly strengthening the group’s hospitality play. The acquisition comes at a time when the group has announced plans to expand its hotel footprint across the country, particularly around airport hubs. With the JAL acquisition, it would also gain ready ownership of JP Hotels, which runs luxury hotel properties under various franchises, giving the group an immediate platform to scale up its presence in the segment. An email sent to Adani Group did not elicit a response till the time of publication. The acquisition of Jaypee’s assets marks one of the most complex insolvency resolutions in recent years, given the breadth of JAL’s assets and the long-drawn nature of the process. The process saw a closely fought contest between the Adani Group and the Anil Agarwal-led Vedanta Group. A key factor in lenders favouring the Adani Group’s bid was the certainty of execution and upfront capital commitment. According to sources, Adani’s proposal includes an upfront payment of Rs 6,000 crore, with the balance to be paid over a relatively shorter two-year period. This contrasted with rival bidder Vedanta’s offer, which involved staggered payments over five years. Anil Agarwal-led Vedanta Ltd has moved the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal, challenging lenders’ approval of Adani Enterprises Ltd’s bid for Jaiprakash Associates Limited. The NCLT’s order also sheds light on why Vedanta’s bid was ultimately rejected. The tribunal said Vedanta submitted a revised addendum to its resolution plan on November 8, 2025, after the 23rd committee of creditors (CoC) meeting on November 7 when final plans had been submitted for voting. The CoC deemed this addendum unsolicited and in violation of the process framework and refused to consider it. Procedural fairness was another key consideration. The CoC noted that accepting Vedanta’s late-stage revision would have required reopening the bidding process and allowing all other bidders to revise offers, which was not feasible within the insolvency timeline. On evaluation metrics as well, Adani’s bid ranked higher. The tribunal noted that independent evaluator BDO assigned Adani an aggregate score of 89.26 out of 100, compared with Vedanta’s 75.60. While Vedanta’s offer was higher on net present value, Adani scored better on overall parameters, including upfront cash and qualitative factors. The tribunal also upheld the CoC’s decision, stating that Vedanta’s addendum could not be considered part of its plan as it violated the process rules, reinforcing the primacy of the CoC’s commercial wisdom in selecting Adani’s bid. Lenders’ preference for Adani’s bid was also shaped by Vedanta’s track record in insolvency-led acquisitions, sources said. The group had earlier withdrawn from the acquisition of GMR Chhattisgarh Energy despite emerging as the highest bidder and later backed out of its approved bid for Ind-Barath Energy (Utkal), triggering litigation. Its acquisition of Meenakshi Energy was also delayed for years before closure. Against this backdrop, Adani’s proposal was seen as offering greater execution certainty. Sources said the plan included Rs 6,000 crore upfront and the full Rs 14,535 crore payout over a shorter two-year period, compared with Vedanta’s staggered payment structure over five years. For lenders seeking to close a resolution process that had already dragged on for years, the upfront cash component and tighter timeline offered greater comfort. Another critical component of the approved plan is the treatment of homebuyers. Nearly Rs 2,074 crore in admitted claims from around 5,000 homebuyers across JAL’s projects have been factored into the resolution, offering long-awaited relief to buyers who have been awaiting possession for nearly a decade. Separately, another Jaypee Group bankruptcy resolution has faced fresh scrutiny after the Enforcement Directorate filed an FIR against the Suraksha Group and its partner, Lakshdeep Investments and Finance, which had acquired Jaypee Infratech Ltd in 2024. Jaypee Infratech owns the Wish Town township in Sector 120 of Noida, involving around 17,000 buyers. The FIR was filed on January 1, 2026. Title: UK court orders SpiceJet to pay $8 million to engine lessor over unpaid rent, maintenance- Moneycontrol.com
Description: London's Commercial Court granted summary judgment on Wednesday in favour of Sunbird France 02 SAS over the unpaid rent and maintenance accruals, finding that SpiceJet had no viable defence.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: A UK court has ordered India's SpiceJet to pay about $8 million to an aircraft engine lessor over unpaid rent and maintenance charges for three engines, in the latest setback for the cash-strapped airline. London's Commercial Court granted summary judgment on Wednesday in favour of Sunbird France 02 SAS over the unpaid rent and maintenance accruals, finding that SpiceJet had no viable defence. The ruling comes as SpiceJet remains under financial strain following the Boeing 737 MAX grounding and COVID-19 pandemic, and losing market share to rivals such as Akasa Air. The unpaid rent dates from January 2022, while maintenance accruals date back to November 2020. The lessor issued default notices in July 2022 and repossessed all three engines between late 2022 and mid-2023. SpiceJet initially hired British solicitors but never filed a defence or response to Sunbird's application, the judgement showed. The airline did not respond to an email seeking comment. Its auditors have warned of uncertainty over the airline's ability to continue as a going concern, citing mounting losses and a gap between current liabilities and assets, according to its latest results. Title: Tata Steel says annual crude steel production in India rises 8% in FY26- Moneycontrol.com
Description: New Delhi, Apr 7 Tata Steel on Tuesday said that it has reported its best ever annual crude steel production at 23.48 million tonnes in India in 2025-2..
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Tata Steel on Tuesday said that it has reported its best ever annual crude steel production at 23.48 million tonnes in India in 2025-26, registering an increase of eight per cent from the preceding fiscal. The increase was mainly on account of the ramp-up of output at its Kalinganagar plant. In the fourth quarter of FY26, crude steel production rose to 6.25 million tonnes from 5.44 MT in the year-ago period, according to the provisional figures of Tata Steel. The annual deliveries in India increased in line with production, reaching 22.53 MT. "Domestic deliveries crossed 20 million tonnes for the first time, highlighting our strong market positioning and enduring customer relationships, Tata Steel said in a filing to BSE. In the fourth quarter of FY26, deliveries were up 10 per cent Y-O-Y, the company said. The automotive and special products vertical recorded the best-ever annual delivery volumes of 3.4 million tonnes, while branded products and the retail segment achieved volumes of 7.3 million tonnes, driven by the performance of well-established brands. Tata Steel further said that the industrial products and projects vertical achieved delivery volumes of 7.2 MT, supported by the performance of value accretive segments. Tata Steel Thailand’s saleable steel production in FY2026 was 1.33 million tonnes, and deliveries were 1.32 million tons. Deliveries improved by 11 per cent YoY, primarily driven by strong domestic rebar sales. Tata Steel group is among the top global steel companies with an annual crude steel capacity of 35 million tonnes. It is one of the world’s most geographically diversified steel producers, with operations and commercial presence across the world. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: On TN poll campaign, Sasikala steps out in 'Jayalalithaa' style- Moneycontrol.com
Description: By concentrating candidates in southern constituencies, her party appears to be banking on identity linkages, local networks, and nostalgia for the earlier AIADMK era.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: On the campaign trail in Tamil Nadu, a familiar image is quietly re-emerging — the carefully draped saree, the measured pauses, the appeal to loyalty, and, above all, the invocation of a towering legacy. At its centre is All India Puratchi Thalaivar Makkal Munnetra Kazhagam (AIPTMMK) chief VK Sasikala, once the closest aide of J Jayalalithaa, now attempting a political reinvention by stepping into the space her mentor once commanded. The former Tamil Nadu Chief Minister often campaigned from her vehicle, with Sasikala seated behind, observing as crowds and media thronged around. Echoing Jayalalithaa’s roadshows, Sasikala now delivers brief speeches from her van across key constituencies in south Tamil Nadu. For decades, Sasikala stood just behind Jayalalithaa — never in the spotlight, but never far from power. As a confidante who lived with the AIADMK chief and managed her inner circle, she reportedly wielded influence without holding office. That proximity became both her greatest asset and her deepest political burden after Jayalalithaa’s death in 2016. After Jayalalithaa’s death in December 2016, Sasikala briefly took control of the AIADMK, being elected as its general secretary and leader of the legislature party. However, in February 2017, she was convicted in a long-pending disproportionate assets case and sentenced to four years in prison, effectively halting her chief ministerial ambitions. She was released in January 2021. On the campaign trail in Tamil Nadu, Sasikala’s current strategy leans heavily on symbolism. From her choice of attire — often echoing Jayalalithaa’s signature style — to her rhetoric centred on “restoring Amma’s rule,” she is consciously invoking nostalgia among AIADMK’s core voters. Her speeches frequently reference Jayalalithaa as “Puratchi Thalaivi” (Revolutionary Leader), framing herself as the rightful custodian of that legacy. The messaging is clear: she is not just another political contender, but the inheritor of an unfinished political project. Even her outreach style mirrors Jayalalithaa’s mass connect politics — direct appeals to women, welfare-centric promises, and an emphasis on emotional loyalty over ideological positioning. "I want people to decide whether they want a rule of betrayers and evil forces or one by selfless leaders,'' she told Times of India at Thiruparankundram in Madurai. According to TOI, on Arappalayam cross road, her party's candidate stood with folded hands on a platform about 300ft away, reminiscent of the way Jayalalithaa's candidates were presented during her campaigns. In her five-minute speeches, Sasikala reportedly takes indirect digs at AIADMK general secretary Edappadi K Palaniswami. Sasikala’s party has fielded candidates in 75 constituencies. Most of them are from southern districts, eyeing Thevar votes. The Thevars are a dominant backward caste group in southern Tamil Nadu, particularly strong in districts such as Madurai, Theni, Ramanathapuram, Sivaganga, and parts of Tirunelveli. Historically, they have been a crucial support base for the AIADMK, especially during the leadership of Jayalalithaa. After her death, however, the party’s internal fragmentation and leadership struggles have led to shifts in these traditional loyalties. Sasikala, who hails from the same social milieu and was once a key power centre in the AIADMK’s inner circle, is now attempting to reassert influence in these regions. By concentrating candidates in southern constituencies, her party appears to be banking on identity linkages, local networks, and nostalgia for the earlier AIADMK era. Title: Om Power Transmission IPO Day 2: Check subscription, GMP trends and other key details- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Om Power Transmission IPO Day 2: The basis of allotment is likely to be finalised on April 15.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Om Power Transmission's IPO enters Day 2 of subscription today, April 10, 2026, after witnessing a steady start on the opening day. The public issue, which opened on April 9, will remain available for bidding until April 13. Also Read: NSE IPO: DRHP may be filed by June-end; listing targeted before December, says CNBC-TV18 On Day 1, the IPO was subscribed 39% overall, led by strong participation from Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs), whose portion was booked 0.78 times (78%). Retail Individual Investors (RIIs) showed moderate interest with 0.30 times (30%) subscription, while Non-Institutional Investors (NIIs) lagged at 0.11 times (11%). Ahead of the issue opening, the company raised Rs 45.01 crore from anchor investors. Mauritius-based Craft Emerging Market Fund PCC, via its Elite Capital Fund and Citadel Capital Fund, picked up 16.57 lakh shares worth over Rs 29 crore. Morgan Stanley Asia (Singapore) acquired 5.71 lakh shares for Rs 10 crore, while Sunrise Investment Trust bought 3.43 lakh shares valued at Rs 6.01 crore. Om Power Transmission operates in the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) segment, focusing on power transmission infrastructure, and has over 14 years of experience in the sector. Also Read: Citius Transnet Investment Trust to open Rs 1,105 crore IPO on April 17 As per its red herring prospectus (RHP), its listed peers include Rajesh Power Services Ltd (P/E of 16.38), Advait Energy Transitions Ltd (P/E of 57.52), and Viviana Power Tech Ltd (P/E of 25.15). The IPO allocation structure earmarks up to 50% of the issue for QIBs, at least 15% for NIIs, and a minimum of 35% for retail investors. In the grey market, the GMP stood at Rs 3.5 as of April 10. Based on the upper price band of Rs 175, the estimated listing price is around Rs 178.5, indicating a modest potential gain of about 2%. The basis of allotment is likely to be finalised on April 15. Refunds are expected to be initiated on April 16, with shares credited to demat accounts on the same day. The stock is tentatively scheduled to list on the BSE and NSE on April 17. Title: OPINION | Leadership is Like Sadhana: A journey of humility, curiosity, and service- Moneycontrol.com
Description: True leadership is not a position. It requires daily humility, honest enquiry, and service. Leaders grow through practice, reflection, and acting for the benefit of others
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: There is a temptation, particularly among those who have reached senior positions, to treat leadership as a destination. You arrive, you lead, and others follow. That is not leadership. It is position. And the difference matters enormously. We live in an age that prizes constant action. Institutions have come to value the energy of the launch, the clarity of the announcement, the confidence of forward motion. These are not wrong instincts. But action has become the primary currency of credibility, and somewhere in the acceleration, reflection has been quietly devalued — not rejected, simply crowded out. The leader who pauses to question, who holds a decision open long enough to examine it honestly, can appear uncertain in a culture that has learned to read speed as competence. And yet it is precisely that pause, that willingness to sit with uncertainty before acting, that separates direction from mere motion. Real leadership is a practice and is hard lifelong work. Like any serious practice, it demands sustained effort, honest self-examination, and a willingness to remain a student long after the world has begun to address you as a teacher. The Bhagavad Gita names this quality precisely. Chapter Four offers a verse that has stayed with me for decades. उपदेक्ष्यन्ति ते ज्ञानं ज्ञानिनस्तत्त्वदर्शिनः।।३२।। Three words carry the full weight of this verse. Pranipata is the posture of reverent humility. Pariprasna is the discipline of honest, searching enquiry. Seva is the commitment to serve. Together, they describe not a moment of learning but a lifelong orientation. They describe sadhana. What three decades of working with tribal communities in the Bandipur forest have taught me could not have been learned in any classroom. The most important lesson was this. The moment you believe you understand a community, the community has already moved beyond your understanding. Humility is not a virtue one cultivates once and then possesses. It is a practice one returns to every day, especially on the days when experience tempts you towards certainty. This is the first discipline of leadership. Lifelong learning begins not with curiosity but with the recognition that knowledge is always partial. Overconfidence narrows perception. It causes a leader to fit new information into old frameworks rather than allowing new information to reshape the framework itself. Humility keeps the frame open. In a world changing as rapidly as ours, that openness is not idealism. It is a survival skill. The second discipline is appreciative enquiry. This phrase is sometimes reduced to a facilitation technique. It is something more foundational. It is a way of approaching reality. Appreciative enquiry asks not what is wrong, but what is working and why. Most organisations are good at diagnosing failure. They are less practiced at understanding success. But it is in pockets of success, even under difficult conditions, that the real intelligence of a system resides. A leader who looks only at what is broken misses the evidence most useful for building what is needed. Appreciative enquiry does not ignore problems. It simply refuses to begin there. A leader who understands the conditions under which people work is better placed to intervene meaningfully. These two disciplines are preparatory. They clear the ground for the third and most essential principle. Everything must serve the good of society. Arjuna’s dialogue with Krishna does not end in philosophical contentment. It ends in action aligned with a larger purpose. The inner work of humility, enquiry, and curiosity is valuable only to the degree that it flows outward into decisions that serve something beyond the leader’s own interest or reputation. This is what the Gita means by nishkama karma. Not the absence of action, but the purification of its motive. For any leader, the test is simple to state and difficult to sustain. Ask, regularly, who benefits from this decision. Ask whether the structures you are building serve the people meant to be served. Ask whether your success requires someone else’s diminishment. These are uncomfortable questions. They are also necessary ones. A leader who stops asking them has stopped growing. What holds all three disciplines together is consistency. It is easy to be humble when you are new. It is harder when you have spent three decades in a domain and someone younger asks a question that unsettles your assumptions. Sadhana is precisely the commitment to return to these disciplines not when they are natural, but when they are effortful. Leadership is not what you demonstrate at your best. It is what you sustain on the ordinary days, through difficult conversations, across decisions that no one will applaud. Arjuna did not enter his dialogue with Krishna as a resolved man. He entered it undone, his bow fallen, his arguments rehearsed but hollow. What followed was not instruction so much as transformation through honest encounter. He asked his questions. He sat with the discomfort of the answers. He moved through his crisis, slowly, toward a clarity that was earned rather than given. That movement is available to each of us. Not as a singular event but as a repeating practice. Every meeting, every decision, every difficult conversation is an opportunity to return to pranipata, pariprasna, and seva. To arrive with humility. To ask honestly. To act in service. The measure of a leader is not found in the scale of what was built or the recognition received. It is found in the quality of attention brought to the ‘work’, in the honesty of the questions asked along the way, and in the degree to which the outcomes served someone other than the leader themselves. That is the sadhana. It does not end. It does not plateau. It simply continues, asking of you, every day, a little more than yesterday. (Dr R Balasubramaniam, an author and is the founder of SVYM and GRAAM and currently serves as Member (Human Resources) at the Capacity Building Commission, Government of India. More about him is at drrbalu.com.) Views are personal, and do not represent the stand of this publication. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: OPINION | Kalpakkam’s fast breeder reactor alone will not lead to nuclear rejuvenation - Moneycontrol.com
Description: The prototype fast breeder reactor reaching criticality is a historic milestone but not sufficient as the national goal is a tenfold increase in capacity by 2047. To realise it, India needs to pursue direct thorium utilisation and simultaneously double down on PHWRs
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The date of 6 April 2026 will be etched in history books as a significant milestone for Indian nuclear establishment. Nearly seven decades after Homi Bhabha conceived the three-stage nuclear power programme, India finally entered the second stage. The 500 MW prototype fast breeder reactor (PFBR) at Kalpakkam finally attained criticality. While this is a welcome development, the resulting euphoria should be tempered by the current state of India’s nuclear programme. In parallel to the three-stage plan, India should pursue reactor technologies that could potentially allow for direct thorium utilisation. Additionally, India should double down on existing first stage reactors, seek modularity in construction and foster closer private sector involvement to rapidly scale up nuclear power in India. Three-stage dream and the long wait for fast breeders When Homi Bhabha conceived the three-stage nuclear dream in the 1950s, he was acutely aware of scant uranium availability in the country. But he also understood the extraordinary energy potential of abundant thorium found in the coastal beach sands. The only problem is that thorium (or Th-232 to be specific) is fertile but not fissile — that is, it cannot be directly fissioned to generate energy. It must first be converted into fissile uranium (U-233) after undergoing a process called transmutation. To exploit thorium given the constraints, Bhabha framed the three-stage nuclear power programme. The first stage involved using pressurised heavy water reactors (PHWRs) which run on natural uranium (containing only 0.7 per cent of fissile U-235). Plutonium (Pu-239) and depleted uranium (U-238) obtained from the spent fuel of the first stage are used for second stage fast breeder reactors (FBRs) that generate more fuel than they consume. A blanket of thorium (Th-232) is introduced around the FBR reactors to obtain the fissile U-233. The U-233 along with Th-232 forms the fuel for running the third stage breeder reactors. The three-stage plan has the potential to generate electricity for decades and even centuries. India has mastered the first stage PHWRs for a few decades now but has struggled with initiating the second stage FBRs. The work on FBRs started in the 1960s, with assistance from France on designing . Construction of a fast breeder test reactor (FBTR) started in the early 1970s, and it began operating in the 1980s. Located in Kalpakkam, the FBTR is a 40 MW thermal sodium cooled fast reactor. While operating the small reactor gave India experience with sodium as a coolant, running the reactor has not been a smooth endeavour over the decades. In 2004, to move work on stage 2 forward, the Indian nuclear establishment started construction of a larger 500 MW PFBR in Kalpakkam. It was expected to reach completion in 2010 but ended up dragging on until the 2020s. Fuel loading finally started in October 2025 and the reactor finally attained criticality in April 2026. Nuclear potential hinges on additional steps It took more than two decades for a single 500 MW PFBR reactor to be completed after construction started. If the history of operating FBTR and other countries’ tryst with FBRs are any indicator, the PFBR might run into technical problems during the initial operational years. This is because handling the coolant in this case, sodium, is very difficult as it reacts exothermically to any air or water exposure. Even if sodium is handled well and the PFBR runs successfully, building a fleet of FBRs will take decades, impeding the exploitation of thorium in the third stage reactors. Therefore, in order to rapidly scale up nuclear power in the country upwards from abysmal roughly 9 GW capacity, India needs to pursue direct thorium utilisation technologies in parallel to the sequential three-stage plan. This means establishing test reactors for different designs such as molten salt reactor, accelerator driven system and high temperature gas cooled reactor. India has mastered the first stage PHWRs and now has the capacity to build and operate 700 MW reactors. New Delhi should double down on PHWRs in close partnership with the private sector, thanks to the Shanti Act of 2025 that dismantled the public sector monopoly. Be it modular deployment (the Bharat Small Modular Reactor or BSMR), captive nuclear plants or large 700 MW plus reactors, PHWRs are a low-hanging fruit for scaling up nuclear power in the country. (Lokendra Sharma is a staff research analyst with the Takshashila Institution’s High-Tech Geopolitics Programme.) Views are personal, and do not represent the stand of this publication. Title: OPINION | BNP powers ahead in Bangladesh; secular-liberals fare well but uncertainty still looms- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Keeping Awami League away tilted matters in Tarique Rahman’s favour. But the controversial referendum on constitutional reforms, which passed, may not get BNP’s support. India needs to note the emergence of a new crop of secular-liberal politicians
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The election is over in Bangladesh. Seat shares are not clear as the declaration of results is unusually delayed. Unofficially, nearly 61 percent votes were polled (Netra News), Tarique Rahman’s Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) is ahead or secured 175+ seats, Jamaat secured 70–80, interim head Dr Muhammad Yunus-backed National Citizen Party (NCP) got six in coalition with Jamaat. The referendum on constitutional reforms passed with a 72 percent majority. Almost all trends were expected. Roughly one-fifth of the country’s electorate is below 25 years and they haven’t seen a semblance of an election in the last 15 years. It was natural that they would come out to vote. The country’s seven to eight percent Hindus voted in large numbers, without which they would have been marked and their sufferings would have gone up. Minorities had little choice beyond BNP and that tilted the results substantially in the party’s favour. That is irrespective of the atrocities and extortion by the party thugs over the last one-and-a-half years. ‘Yes’ vote won in the referendum because it was made to win. In the past military rulers sought approval to stay through Yes and No votes. Yunus sought blanket approval to over three-dozen contentious issues, which are “too complicated for most to understand,” alongside the election fervour “in an opaque and non-serious manner,” claimed leading economist and political scientist Prof Rehman Sobhan (The Daily Star, 30 January 2026). While parties barely spoke about it, the whole administration was geared up to get the referendum passed. The Election Commission initially kept watching as the entire interim administration starting from Dr Yunus was openly campaigning for ‘Yes’ votes. By the time they ordered a clampdown, even the school teachers were pressed into service. As it emerged yesterday, the opacity was maintained even in the ballot for the referendum. The referendum result may run into BNP opposition Interestingly, the ground rules set by the referendum suggest that the new Parliament will act as a constituent assembly for six months to implement all the proposals. BNP is unlikely to accept that and many expect that the problem will start from there. A political consensus for a two-tier Parliament is expected as it can accommodate more faces. What happens to others will depend on how BNP’s relation with its one-time coalition partner Jamaat evolves. Bangladesh’s electorate extends support to Liberation history and some secular-liberals The election had thrown up a few instances of secular-liberal resilience which Delhi must keep note of and invest in. Advocate Rumeen Farhana was denied nomination by BNP. She contested as an independent and won from a constituency that is adjacent to Tripura. The daughter of a top leader of the freedom movement, Farhana was a distinct critic of Yunus over the last year. She had openly criticized the attack on Liberation history and some of her observations went in favour of Hasina. The biggest and the most memorable win of 2026 was of senior advocate Fazlur Rahman for BNP. A staunch Mujib loyalist and a fiery orator, he was once frustrated by the Awami League and ended up as an adviser to Khaleda Zia. After Hasina’s ouster, he emerged as the strongest voice speaking for Liberation, Mujibur Rahman and the rich history of the League. He has zero tolerance for Jamaat and has been direct in his attacks on the anti-Liberation and Islamist forces. Over the last year, he was threatened with mobbing. BNP suspended him once but he was unrelenting. When he finally got the ticket, a section of the Awami League, reportedly closer to the former President Mohammad Abdul Hamid, tried their best to defeat him. Hamid’s brother-in-law contested for Jamaat from Fazlur’s constituency. Yet, Fazlur Rahman won by a landslide. India cannot afford to be nostalgic about Hasina Rumeen and Fazlur are not alone. There are BNP candidates like Md Ali Asghar Lobby of Khulna who protected Hindus. Dr Manisha Chakraborty of the Bangladesh Socialist Party lost the election but she is surely a face to reckon with. The fall of Hasina was a blessing in disguise. It helped throw up new faces at the front. It is time India dumps the Pranab (Mukherjee) doctrine and invests in faces and the future outside the party lines. The fall of the Sheikh Hasina government of the Awami League came through a combination of a pile-up of dissent from internal failures and admitted orchestration of protests by Jamaat. Outside influences like admitted arm-twisting of the army by the UN human rights body paved the way to her ouster. Over the last one and a half years, Jamaat enjoyed maximum grip over the administration. They also established strong control over the media and dictated public discourse. Outrageous claims were made on national history. Jamaat-backed bloggers sitting abroad played a key role in spreading the fear of mobbing discordant voices. The administration helped it by either keeping silent or jailing targeted voices. This election – held without Awami League – was their golden opportunity to acquire power. There were indications of tacit Western support – particularly from the US and UK – behind Jamaat. Delhi backed BNP. The headroom was artificial but Jamaat failed to turn the opportunity into a reality. They ended up with three to four times more seats compared to the previous best of 18 in 1991. Jamaat offered outside support to BNP’s Khaleda Zia government that year. Re-entry of Awami League will change political dynamics These results will change once Awami League is back into the fray in the next election. If everything goes well, the stranded Awami activists in India might return to the country this year. The fate of Hasina is a separate issue. The League has not conducted itself well during the interim rule. On the one side, they were hounded by an extremely vicious disguised political disposition in power. On the other hand, the party’s internal reforms are overdue. Most of those senior leaders stationed in India are unwanted by their own grassroots workers. They are a liability both to India and the League. Hasina knows that and it was last heard that she avoids the company (virtual) of senior leaders for the grassroots workers. League’s stand on the election is not beyond criticism either. A ‘No boat (Awami symbol), no vote’ call from outside ended up helping the landslide win of the referendum. Roughly 10 percent of Bangladeshi voters are reportedly stationed abroad and the dominant majority did not participate in the election, indicating diaspora’s support to the League. While the judiciary caved in, Army ensured polling was fair Bangladesh army ensured a fair poll but the ground was not fair. Huge pressure was created on Jatiya Party and they could not create a mark in their stronghold of the greater Rangpur area. The benefits went to Jamaat. There was perceived backroom engineering to gift seats to student leaders of NCP. Hasnat Abdullah, a face from the 2024 protest days, won by a record margin from a Comilla seat because the high court clamped down on the BNP candidate for bank default. That is despite over three dozen other questionable characters having passed the net. The state control over the courts reached an unprecedented high during the Yunus era, with acquittal of favourites and detention of critics becoming a norm. Despite a successful election, Bangladesh enters an uncertain era Overall, this is not an election to remember. Bangladesh will surely enter a new era of uncertainty. Tarique Rahman must erase his past and establish himself as a good administrator. The law-and-order situation had been the worst for the last 18–19 months. He must restore it. That requires support of the police. It is to be seen if Rahman revokes the (illegal) indemnity granted by Yunus to crimes by the 2024 protesters. Three fourths of the country’s police stations were destroyed. Nearly half a dozen jails were emptied. And over 4000 sophisticated arms were looted. At least 1500 of them are untraceable. Title: Michael Burry holds firm on Palantir short despite Trump boost- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Burry described the stock as "wildly overvalued" and argued its fundamental worth is below $50 a share — less than half its current trading price
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Famed investor Michael Burry is maintaining his bearish bet against Palantir Technologies even as a social media endorsement from President Donald Trump gave the stock a temporary lift. Burry, who rose to prominence for predicting the 2008 housing market collapse, disclosed in a Substack post that he continues to hold long-dated put options on the AI software company — a position he first established in late 2025 and has since rolled over multiple times. He currently holds put options expiring in December 2026 and June 2027, at strike prices of $100 and $50 respectively, and said he has no intention of unwinding the trade. The disclosure came on the same day Trump praised Palantir on Truth Social, calling the company's warfighting capabilities "great" and urging skeptics to ask the country's enemies for proof. The post helped stabilise the stock after a steep intraday decline, though Palantir still ended the week down nearly 14%, extending its year-to-date losses to around 28%, CNBC reported. The stock closed Friday at $128.06. Burry described the stock as "wildly overvalued" and argued its fundamental worth is below $50 a share — less than half its current trading price, according to CNBC. While he conceded the Trump post could spark a short-term rally, he said that would not change his conviction. Palantir has been seen as a beneficiary of the ongoing Iran conflict given its deep ties to the US military and intelligence community. Under the current administration, the company has continued winning government contracts and expanding its Pentagon work, with CEO Alex Karp staying closely engaged with the White House. In a separate disclosure, Burry said he also added to his bearish position on Nvidia, purchasing January 2027 put options. He noted that while high implied volatility made an outright short tempting, he preferred the defined-loss structure of options and flagged the possibility of rolling the position before time decay becomes a meaningful factor. Karp previously dismissed Burry's wagers against both companies as "super weird" and "bats--- crazy." Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Malls, high-streets in NCR clock 45% rise in leasing of retail spaces in January-March: C&W- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Real estate consultant Cushman & Wakefield's data showed that leasing of retail spaces in Delhi-NCR stood at 0.59 million square feet in January-March, as against 0.41 million square feet in the year-ago period.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Leasing of retail spaces at shopping malls and high streets in Delhi-NCR rose 45 per cent to nearly 6 lakh sq ft in January-March on better demand, according to Cushman & Wakefield. Real estate consultant Cushman & Wakefield's data showed that leasing of retail spaces in Delhi-NCR stood at 0.59 million square feet in January-March, as against 0.41 million square feet in the year-ago period. Out of the total leasing in Delhi-NCR, shopping malls accounted for 64 per cent of total volumes, while high streets contributed the remaining 36 per cent. Among India's top eight cities, Delhi-NCR led leasing activity with a 30 per cent share, supported by sustained demand across both malls and main streets, the consultant said. Overall, the leasing of retail spaces across the top eight cities fell 10 per cent to 1.95 million square feet in January-March from 2.17 million sq ft in the corresponding period of the preceding year. The fall was mainly due to supply constraints. In the 2025 calendar year, the leasing stood at 9.21 million sq ft in the top eight cities -- Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune, Bengaluru, Kolkata and Ahmedabad -- driven mainly by demand from fashion, and food and beverages (F&B) segments. Gautam Saraf, Executive Managing Director - Mumbai & New Business, Cushman & Wakefield, said, "India's retail real estate market is currently operating in a demand-led environment, where occupier interest continues to outpace the availability of high-quality retail space." The demand is being driven by sustained expansion from domestic retailers, increased participation from international brands, and a clear shift towards organised, professionally managed retail formats, he added. S K Sayal, MD & CEO of Bharti Real Estate, said, "Retail leasing in India is witnessing robust momentum, supported by strong fundamentals such as rising consumer confidence, evolving lifestyles, and the growth of experience-led formats." Urban infrastructure and connectivity continue to play a critical role in enhancing the appeal of retail destinations, he added. Ankit Sharma, SVP-Leasing, Elan Group, said the underlying demand for organised retail continues to remain strong across key markets, supported by sustained consumption trends and the expanding presence of both domestic and global brands. Robin Mangla, President of M3M India, said the demand for retail spaces remains strong in Delhi-NCR. With new supply in the pipeline, he said, the leasing activities would further strengthen. Shriram P M Monga, Co-founder at consulting firm SRED, said the robust leasing performance in NCR speaks volumes about the strength of the story of consumption in India. "There is a definite preference towards quality retail spaces, organised and in established malls and high streets. Despite a lack of supply, leasing has been healthy in the sector, and it goes without saying that there is a willingness on the occupier side to act fast for the right assets," Monga added. Title: Kerala records 78.03% voter turnout in assembly polls, 4% higher than 2021- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The ruling National Democratic Alliance has built its campaign around infrastructure development, while also pushing the contentious issue of a Uniform Civil Code and maintaining a strong stance on border security and 'infiltration'.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: As Kerala went to the polls, it recorded a voter turnout of 78.03% till 5pm on Thursday. The crucial elections will decide whether the ruling Lelf Democratic Front (LDF) will get a straight third term, or if the United Democratic Front (UDF) makes a comeback, or if BJP springs a surprise in an otherwise bipolar battle. In the 2021 Kerala Assembly polls, a turnout of 74.06 per cent was recorded and the highest polling the state has seen is 80.5 per cent which was in the 1987 Assembly elections. The ruling LDF is fighting to retain power, banking on Chief Minister Pinarayi Vijayan 's model of governance and welfare. The Congress-led UDF is vying to regain power, seeking to end a decade of Left rule in the state. Amid this traditionally bipolar contest, the BJP-led NDA is looking to disrupt the duopoly of LDF and UDF and aims to translate its growing vote share into a decisive presence in the state assembly. The polling appeared to be slowing down slightly in the afternoon as compared to the first half of the day when there was an increase of around 16 per cent in the turnout every two hours. The turnout was recorded at 16.23 per cent at 9 am, which increased to 33.28 per cent at 11 am and 49.70 per cent at 1 pm, according to EC figures. However, at 3pm, turnout of 62.71 per cent was recorded and it increased to 75.01 per cent at 5 pm, indicating an increase of around 13 per cent. The figures also showed that Kozhikode district recorded the highest polling percentage of 77.63 per cent and pushed Ernakulam, which had been in the front since morning, to second place with 77.25 per cent at 5 pm. Constituency-wise, Kunnathunad topped with 81.99 per cent. The lowest turnout came in Kaduthuruthy with 66.81 per cent (all 5 pm data). Political leaders and prominent personalities who cast their votes in the morning included CM Vijayan, Congress leader V D Satheesan, Union Minister of State Suresh Gopi, KPCC chief Sunny Joseph and actor Mohanlal. Title: Kerala Elections 2026 Live: Voter turnout of 75.01% till 5 pm; Congress MP Shashi Tharoor says 'BJP not a factor'
Description: The election is a crucial contest, with the ruling Left Democratic Front (LDF) aiming for a historic third consecutive term under chief minister Pinarayi Vijayan, in power since 2016
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: See the top gainers, losers, invest and get updated what's happening in the crypto market Discover the secret world of unlisted shares — analyse India's top pre-IPO companies and invest before they hit the markets The Kerala Legislative Assembly elections are being held today, April 9, 2026, in a single phase covering all 140 constituencies. The counting of votes is scheduled for May 4. Kerala Assembly Election 2026: Polling status by constituency Assembly Elections 2026: State Legislative Assembly Speaker Shamseer casts his vote Kerala State Legislative Assembly Speaker AN Shamseer casts his vote. He is a CPI(M) legislator representing Thalassery. Assembly Elections 2026: Polling peaceful and very progressive, says State Chief Electoral Officer State Chief Electoral Officer, Rathan U Kelkar says, “The election in Keralam has been very peaceful and very progressive. At this point in time, we have crossed around 40% of our polling percentage. Looking at the trend, we are very hopeful… Assembly Elections 2026: Mohanlal votes in Kerala elections, urges citizens to participate Superstar Mohanlal on Thursday cast his vote in the ongoing Kerala Legislative Assembly elections and urged citizens to fulfil their democratic responsibility. Sharing a selfie from his car, the actor displayed the indelible ink mark on his index finger and called on voters across the state to participate. The actor voted at a school in the Nemom constituency in Thiruvananthapuram. "Done my part. Your turn, Keralam go vote!" he wrote on his official X handle, encouraging people to exercise their franchise. Kerala Assembly Election 2026: Puthuppally MLA Chandy Oommen casts vote, says Keralam backing Congress Puthuppally MLA Chandy Oommen on Thursday cast his vote in Kottayam as voting is underway for the Keralam assembly election, saying voters are backing Congress to seek justice for the alleged harassment of his father, Oommen Chandy. Speaking to ANI, Oommen said, "When this government was in opposition, they carried out a witch hunt against my father. They are protecting the people who carried out the witch hunt. They did not take any action." "The CBI also concluded that my father was not guilty. Entire Keralam is voting against this government. Today is the day when my father will get justice by giving the Congress government to the state," said the son of the former Chief Minister of Kerala, who passed away in 2023. He came dressed in a black outfit to vote and was accompanied by his mother and sister. The Puthuppally seat holds significant political importance for the Congress, being a traditional stronghold of the party. Oommen, who won the 2023 Puthyppally bypoll, was necessitated after his father's death. is seeking to retain the constituency. Kerala Elections 2026:4.6 lakh first-time voters, says state chief electoral officer Kerala’s Chief Electoral Officer Rathan U Kelkar said the polling process in the state has been peaceful and smooth, with turnout crossing 40% so far. He expressed hope that participation could reach the 90% mark by the end of voting, which began at 7 a.m. and will conclude at 6 p.m. Kelkar also noted strong enthusiasm among new voters, adding that the electoral roll includes around 4.6 lakh first-time voters who have turned out in significant numbers. Kerala Elections 2026: UDF will come to power with thumping majority, says Congress Congress leader MM Hassan says, "UDF will come to power with a thumping majority…Within 24 hours of counting (of votes), the party high command will announce (name of CM). Kerala Elections 2026: Left Front coming back to power, says CPI(M)'s John Brittas as voting underway As Keralam votes, Communist Party of India (Marxist) MP John Brittas on Thursday expressed confidence that the Left Front will return to power. Speaking to the reporters after casting his vote at Jawahar Nagar LP School here, Brittas said, "It is clear that the Left Front is going to come back to power. There is an overwhelming response from the people... People are enthusiastic to participate in this democratic process... Left front government did an unparalleled performance this time." He accused the RSS and Jamat-e-Islami of promoting religious polarisation but expressed confidence that Keralam voters will not fall into this trap. "There is no difference between the RSS and Jamat E Islami, because both are proponents of religious theocratic governance in the state and clap their hands together to ensure there is a polarisation, but the people of Keralam will not fall in this trap," he said. Kerala Election News: Man dies after casting vote in Thrissur A 62-year-old man collapsed and passed away after casting his vote at Vaniampara in Thrissur on Thursday. The deceased was identified as Vinodan, a native of Ramanchira near Vaniampara, police told PTI. According to police, Vinodan had reached EKM UP School in Vaniampara in the morning to cast his vote. After standing in the queue for nearly an hour, he exercised his franchise. While coming out of the polling station, he suddenly collapsed at around 10.45 am, police said. A case will be registered and investigation initiated into the incident, police said. Kerala Elections 2026: Actor Mammootty, his wife Sulfath Kutty cast their vote Actor Mammootty and his wife Sulfath Kutty cast their vote at a polling station in Kochi today. Kerala Elections 2026: Congress MP Shashi Tharoor says fight between incumbent LDF vs challenging UDF Congress MP Shashi Tharoor has said that the BJP is not a factor in the Kerala Assembly polls. Speaking to ANI, he said, "This is a state where the BJP is a zero-seat party in the Assembly. To even mention them in the same breath is an excessive compliment. It is a fight between an incumbent LDF govt and a challenging UDF." Title: India’s forex reserves jump by $9 billion to $697.12 billion: RBI- Moneycontrol.com
Description: In the previous reporting week, which ended on March 27, the overall reserves had dropped by $10.288 billion to $688.058 billion.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: India's forex reserves jumped by $9.063 billion to $697.121 billion for the week ended April 3, 2026, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) said on Friday. In the previous reporting week, which ended on March 27, the overall reserves had dropped by $10.288 billion to $688.058 billion. The forex kitty had expanded to an all-time high of $728.494 billion during the week ended February 27 this year, before the onset of the West Asia crisis, which led to several weeks of depletion. The rupee has come under pressure since the start of the West Asia conflict, and the RBI has been intervening in the forex market through dollar sales and had to take some surprising policy measures to harness the fall. For the week ended April 3, foreign currency assets, a major component of the reserves, increased by $1.784 billion to $552.856 billion, the central bank's data showed. Expressed in dollar terms, the foreign currency assets include the effects of appreciation or depreciation of non-US units, such as the euro, pound, and yen, held in the foreign exchange reserves. Value of gold reserves increased by $7.221 billion to $120.742 billion during the week, the RBI said. The Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) were up $58 million to $18.707 billion, the apex bank said. India's reserve position with the IMF was unchanged at $4.816 billion at the end of the reporting week, according to the apex bank's data. Title: India's Bloomberg bond index entry 'not a closed chapter', engagement continues- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Discussions with Bloomberg 'active' even after January deferment as government favours calibrated opening
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: India’s inclusion in the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index remains under consideration and the “chapter has not been closed” following January's deferment , a government source has said. Engagement with the index provider continues even in the “in the current environment”, the official added. Bloomberg had in January deferred India’s inclusion after a review, citing operational and market infrastructure considerations despite expectations of an imminent entry. “On the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index, they had almost completed the process and given every indication but in January they held back. The chapter has not been closed, so we will have to see how it evolves going forward,” the official said, indicating that the decision is being treated as a pause rather than a rejection. Inclusion in the index is being seen as a milestone for India, as it can attract large passive inflows from global funds tracking the benchmark, deepen the domestic bond market, and potentially lower borrowing costs for the government over time. The government indicated that discussions with Bloomberg remain active despite the setback. India continues to work with stakeholders to address outstanding issues, the official said. “It is certainly not a closed chapter, even from India’s perspective. We are continuing to engage and work together to see what can be done in the current environment,” the official said. Evolving global financial conditions, alongside domestic policy considerations, are influencing the pace and sequencing of further steps towards inclusion. The dialogue with Bloomberg suggests that technical discussions are still underway. Inclusion will depend on how India addresses the identified gaps while balancing risks associated with capital flow volatility. India’s authorities are wary of moving too quickly in easing access to foreign investors. “The effort is to understand how far we can go in terms of opening up. That is the key question -- the extent and pace at which further steps can be taken,” the official said. “We are trying to open up steadily but without opening the floodgates. You do not want a situation where you liberalise too quickly and then have to roll back later.” The government’s stance is a preference for incremental and durable reforms rather than aggressive liberalisation aimed solely at meeting index inclusion criteria. “The idea is to move forward in a calibrated and durable manner, ensuring that each step is sustainable. So, we will have to see how this progresses,” the official said. Bloomberg held back India’s entry into its flagship global bond index in January, citing operational and market infrastructure considerations. Issues related to post-trade processes, settlement systems and ease of access for foreign investors were among the factors flagged during consultations. While India meets key eligibility criteria such as market size and liquidity, these operational gaps have delayed inclusion in the index, which tracks investment-grade government bonds globally and is widely followed by passive investors. India has eased access to its bond market in recent years, including through the Fully Accessible Route introduced by the Reserve Bank of India, which allows unrestricted foreign investment in select government securities. The country has already been included in major emerging market bond indices, including JPMorgan’s Government Bond Index–Emerging Markets and Bloomberg’s emerging market local currency index. But entry into the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index is seen as a significant next step that could attract large passive inflows. By emphasising a calibrated approach and continued engagement, the government seems to signal that integration with global markets will proceed on its own terms even if it means a longer timeline. Title: Goyal holds talks with Saudi trade minister on strengthening supply chains- Moneycontrol.com
Description: The commerce ministry, in a statement, said that Goyal had a virtual call with Qassabi.
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal on Saturday held discussions with Saudi Arabia's Trade Minister Majid bin Abdullah Al Qassabi on ways to strengthen supply chains disrupted due to the West Asia crisis, after holding talks with his counterparts from the UAE, Bahrain and Kuwait. All these countries are members of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) with which India is negotiating a free trade agreement. The commerce ministry, in a statement, said that Goyal had a virtual call with Qassabi. "The Ministers reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening bilateral trade and expressed optimism for early progress in the India-GCC FTA negotiations," it said. Both sides noted the strain conflict has put on regional supply chains and stressed the need for an early recovery through coordinated efforts to ensure smooth trade flows, it said. Goyal highlighted India's steps to support exports to Saudi Arabia and the Gulf region. The joint attack launched by the US and Israel on Iran had led to severe disruptions in the movement of ships in international waters, particularly to the West Asian nations. The conflict has posed challenges for exporters to ship goods to the Gulf region, with which India had a bilateral trade of USD 178 billion in 2024-25 (USD 56.87 billion exports and USD 121.67 billion imports). The major sectors which are under stress include petroleum products, chemicals and plastics, engineering goods, rice, pharma and gems and jewellery. The six countries in this region are the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait. Discover the latest Business News , Sensex , and Nifty updates. Title: Diesel export duty hiked to Rs 55.5 per litre; ATF levy raised to Rs 42- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Petrol, however, has been left untouched, with its export duty holding at nil
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: The government has more than doubled the export duty on diesel, hiking it from Rs 21.5 per litre to Rs 55.5 per litre on Saturday, while simultaneously raising the levy on Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF) from Rs 29.5 per litre to Rs 42 per litre. Petrol, however, has been left untouched, with its export duty holding at nil — a signal that domestic petrol supplies are considered adequate for now. The steep revision reflects government's continuing effort to curb the export of refined fuels at the expense of domestic availability, particularly as global energy prices remain volatile. Government sources said that the export duty hike was not about boosting revenue for the government, but "it is more about not allowing exporters to take undue advantage due to price differences." This hike would further lessen the pressure on Centre's finances -- which has been facing a loss of Rs 5,500 crore every fortnight due to the excise duty cuts. The government's move is to shield the aviation sector from a potential price shock. Following a hike in the windfall tax on Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF), officials are reviewing tax structures to ensure rising fuel costs don’t translate into higher airfares, as per sources. The hike is an effort to stabilize ticket prices for passengers and protect airline margins amidst ongoing volatility in global oil markets. Also, this windfall fall will not apply fuel refined in Special Economic Zones (SEZ). The move is part of the broader windfall tax framework, under which the government periodically adjusts levies on fuel exports to balance refiner profitability against local market needs. Title: Circulate Capital raises $220 million for Asia Fund II, to step up recycling investments- Moneycontrol.com
Description: Circulate Capital says its has added about 900,000 tonnes of annual recycling capacity since 2020 and also executed exits in companies such as India-based digital waste management company Recykal
Author: Moneycontrol
Content: Circulate Capital has raised $220 million for the first close of its second Asia-focused fund, crossing 70 percent of its $300 million target and exceeding the size of its maiden fund, Singapore-based investment firm said in a statement on April 1. The fund raise highlights growing investor appetite for circular economy investments. The firm plans to deploy the capital across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Indonesia, Thailand and Vietnam, targeting businesses in recycling, packaging and circular supply chains. “Circulate Capital is the first and only private markets manager to bet exclusively on circular supply chains across South and Southeast Asia. Our track record of successful exits demonstrates that the circular economy is no longer just a subset of ESG or sustainability. It is a sophisticated asset class that can deliver liquidity to private equity investors,” Circulate Capital Founder and CEO Rob Kaplan said. The fund will focus on scaling plastic recycling, building capacity in newer materials such as polyolefins and investing in alternative packaging and electronics recycling. The round saw participation of a mix of returning corporate backers such as Coca-Cola Company, Danone, Dow and Procter & Gamble and development finance institutions, including the International Finance Corporation and British International Investment. New investors include the Emerging Markets Climate Action Fund, European and Australian public institutions, and several family offices. “With Fund II, we are ready to scale and capture the massive growth potential inherent in these high-velocity economies, to build businesses that deliver financial and impact returns for our investors,” Kaplan said. The fund aims to finance nearly two million tonnes of recycling capacity over time, reflecting growing investor interest in circular economy solutions amid regulatory changes, supply chain volatility and rising raw material costs. Globally, only a small portion of materials is reused, highlighting the scale of the opportunity in recycling infrastructure. Circulate Capital said its earlier fund has already built a track record in the region, adding about 900,000 tonnes of annual recycling capacity since 2020 and executing exits in companies such as Recykal, an India-based digital waste management company. The firm’s strategy focuses on backing businesses that cater to both domestic demand and multinational supply chains, while formalising recycling operations in emerging markets. With Asia continuing to generate significant waste volumes alongside rapid consumption growth, investors are increasingly viewing circular supply chains as a scalable investment theme that can deliver both financial returns and environmental outcomes, the statement said.
